articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: Fresh-Picked Weekends: Your Family Guide to the 2026 Omaha Farmers Market
articles.body: There is something quietly wonderful about a weekend morning spent wandering rows of canopy tents, basket in hand, soaking up the smell of fresh bread and the colors of just-picked flowers. For generations of Omaha families, that morning has a name: the Omaha Farmers Market. Each year from late spring through the heart of fall, this beloved outdoor tradition fills two of the city's favorite gathering spots with farm-fresh produce, homemade treats, local makers, and the easy, friendly energy that only a neighborhood market can offer. Whether you are a longtime regular or planning your very first visit, the 2026 season is a wonderful excuse to slow down and enjoy a morning together. Here is the simplest way to think about it: there are two markets, on two days, in two parts of town. The Saturday market sets up downtown in the historic Old Market at 11th and Jackson Streets, running from 8:00am to 12:30pm. The Sunday market gathers in Aksarben Village at 67th and Mercy Road, open a little later from 9:00am to 1:00pm. For 2026, the Saturday Old Market runs May 2 through October 10, and the Sunday Aksarben Village market runs May 3 through October 11. Both markets happen every single weekend in between, rain or shine, which means you can build a standing family tradition all summer and right on through the crisp, apple-scented weeks of fall. With the season carrying into September and October, there is still plenty of time to make a morning of it. The real magic, of course, is in the strolling. You will find tables stacked with sweet corn, ripe tomatoes, leafy greens, berries, peppers, and whatever else is in season that week, much of it picked just a day or two before. There are bakers with warm bread and flaky pastries, vendors selling local honey, jam, cheese, roasted nuts, kettle corn, and mini donuts, and buckets overflowing with fresh-cut sunflowers and zinnias that make the perfect centerpiece for Sunday dinner. Hungry little ones can nibble on breakfast treats while you sip coffee, and you will often catch the gentle sound of live music drifting between the tents. Many weekends you will spot makers offering handmade soaps, candles, pottery, and crafts, so a quick walk can easily turn into finding a small treasure to take home. It is an especially lovely outing for families because it asks so little and gives so much. There is no admission fee to browse and wander, the pace is unhurried, and there is room for children to walk, look, and learn where their food actually comes from. Striking up a conversation with the farmer who grew your tomatoes, letting a child pick out the prettiest bunch of flowers, or simply sharing a warm pastry on a bench together makes for the kind of simple, screen-free morning that sticks in a child's memory. Both markets welcome leashed pets too, so the family dog is invited to come along for the walk. A few friendly tips will make your visit even smoother. Try to arrive early, especially if there is a particular vendor or produce you have your heart set on, because the freshest items and the best parking go quickly. Bring along a reusable tote or two and a little cash; while many vendors now take cards, cash keeps things simple, and both market locations have an on-site ATM if you need it. Parking is easy at both spots: in Aksarben Village the nearby garages are free, and downtown the Park Omaha Jackson Garage offers a free first hour. Strollers and wagons roll along just fine, so pack water, sunscreen, and a hat for the kids, and give yourselves time to linger rather than rush. Part of what makes these markets feel so special is the way they fit into a bigger, well-loved part of the city. A Saturday trip pairs beautifully with a wander through the cobblestone charm of the Old Market district, and if you are gathering ideas for a budget-friendly summer, the market belongs right alongside our roundup of free things to do in Omaha this summer. It is the kind of outing that feels generous to your wallet and your weekend alike. So set the alarm a touch early, gather everyone up, and make your way to one of the markets this season. For the most up-to-date details on dates, hours, special events, and the latest vendor lineup, visit the official website at omahafarmersmarket.com. Fresh flowers, friendly faces, and a basket full of the good stuff are waiting. We will see you under the tents.
articles.excerpt: From May through October, the Omaha Farmers Market turns two beloved spots into a weekend tradition of fresh produce, local makers, and friendly faces. Saturdays in the Old Market and Sundays in Aksarben Village offer a wholesome, easygoing outing the whole family will love. Here is everything you need to plan your visit.

articles.title: Hiring Your First Employee: The Legal Steps Owners Overlook
articles.body: Hiring your first employee is one of the proudest moments in the life of a business. It means you have grown beyond what your own two hands can do, and someone is willing to stake part of their livelihood on what you have built. It is also the moment your legal responsibilities quietly multiply. Becoming an employer is not just a bigger version of being self-employed; it brings a set of obligations many owners do not see coming. Knowing them in advance turns a stressful scramble into an orderly, confident step forward. The first practical task is to make your business official as an employer in the eyes of the government. That generally starts with obtaining an Employer Identification Number, the federal tax ID that identifies your business much as a Social Security number identifies a person. You will also typically need to register with your state's tax and labor agencies, because hiring an employee creates obligations at both the federal and state level. These registrations are not difficult, but skipping them is the kind of oversight that creates problems later, so they belong at the very top of the checklist. Next comes a question that sounds simple but carries real weight: is this person actually an employee, or an independent contractor? Owners often assume they can choose, when in fact the law looks at the true nature of the working relationship, including how much control you have over how and when the work is done. Getting this wrong, by treating a true employee as a contractor, is one of the most expensive mistakes a new employer can make, exposing you to back taxes and penalties. Because the stakes are high, it is worth understanding the distinction carefully, a topic explored in our companion piece on the difference between contractors and employees. Once you have a genuine employee, the paperwork begins, and it is more than a formality. New employees generally complete forms verifying their eligibility to work and their tax withholding, and as an employer you are responsible for keeping certain records and meeting reporting requirements. Many states also require you to report new hires to a designated agency within a set window. None of this is glamorous, but each piece exists for a reason, and handling it correctly from the first hire builds habits that will serve you through every employee who follows. Then there are the ongoing obligations that come with having people on your payroll. As an employer, you are generally responsible for withholding taxes from wages and paying employer taxes of your own. Most employers are also required to carry workers' compensation coverage, which protects both the employee and you if someone is hurt on the job, and to comply with wage and hour laws covering minimum pay and overtime. Many workplaces must display certain labor law notices where employees can see them. These requirements vary by location and by the size of your business, which is exactly why they are so easy to overlook and so important to get right. Underneath the compliance checklist lies a simple truth: an employee is a person who is counting on you, and the law reflects the seriousness of that relationship. Treating the obligations as a burden to dodge tends to create exactly the disputes and penalties owners fear. Treating them as the basic responsibilities of being a good employer, and setting them up properly from the start, protects your business, your employee, and the working relationship between you. Done well, this foundation lets you focus on the part that matters most, which is building a team. Because the rules differ from place to place and change over time, the first hire is a moment when a little professional guidance pays for itself many times over. Having affordable, ongoing access to legal counsel who can help you understand your obligations as a new employer, put the right agreements and policies in place, and answer questions as your team grows is exactly what a small business plan through LegalShield is designed to provide. To learn more: click HERE. Take the legal steps seriously from your very first hire, and you turn a daunting milestone into a strong foundation for everything that comes next.

articles.title: Do You Really Need an LLC? Choosing the Right Structure for Your Business
articles.body: If you have started a business or are thinking about it, you have almost certainly heard the same three letters repeated like a mantra: LLC. Form an LLC, everyone says, as if it were a magic shield you buy once and never think about again. But the real question is not whether LLCs are good. It is whether one is right for you, what it actually does, and what it does not. Understanding that is the difference between protection you can count on and a false sense of security. Start with the structure most people already have without realizing it: the sole proprietorship. The moment you start doing business on your own and do nothing else, you are a sole proprietor by default. It is simple, cheap, and requires almost no paperwork, which is exactly why so many people begin there. But it carries one enormous catch. In a sole proprietorship, there is no legal separation between you and your business. If the business is sued or runs up debts it cannot pay, your personal assets, your savings, your car, even your home, can be on the line. The same is true of a general partnership, which is essentially a sole proprietorship with more than one owner, and which can also make you responsible for what your partner does. This is the gap the limited liability company is designed to close. An LLC creates a separate legal entity that stands between your business and your personal life. If the company is sued or cannot pay its debts, generally only the business's assets are exposed, and your personal property stays protected. That single feature, the liability shield, is the main reason the LLC has become the default choice for small business owners. It also offers flexibility in how you are taxed and far less formality than a corporation, making it a comfortable middle ground for most. Then there is the corporation, and a tax election you have probably heard mentioned in the same breath: the S corporation. A corporation is a more formal, more structured entity, traditionally favored by businesses that plan to bring on investors or eventually go public. The S corporation is not a separate kind of entity so much as a tax status that an LLC or corporation can elect, one that can, in the right circumstances, reduce self-employment taxes for owners who pay themselves a salary. These options can be powerful, but they come with more rules, more paperwork, and more reasons to have professional guidance before you choose. So how do you decide? The honest answer is that it depends on your risk, your income, and your plans. If your business has almost no chance of being sued and earns very little, a sole proprietorship may be perfectly reasonable to start. But the moment you have real liability exposure, employees, valuable assets, meaningful income, or partners, the protection of an LLC usually becomes well worth the modest cost and effort. And as your profits grow, a conversation about whether an S corporation election makes sense can save you real money. The key is to match the structure to your actual situation rather than copying whatever a friend did. It is also worth saying clearly what an LLC does not do. It does not protect you if you personally guarantee a loan, if you commit fraud, or if you fail to treat the business as genuinely separate by mixing personal and business funds. The shield only holds if you respect it. Forming the entity is the first step; running it properly is what keeps the protection alive. Choosing a business structure is one of those decisions that looks like simple paperwork but quietly shapes your taxes, your liability, and your future for years. It is exactly the kind of question worth running past a professional before you commit, when getting it right is easy and cheap, rather than after a problem appears, when it is neither. Having affordable, ongoing access to legal counsel who can look at your specific situation and help you choose and set up the right structure is exactly what a small business plan through LegalShield is built to provide. To learn more: click HERE. Pick the structure that fits the business you actually have, set it up correctly, and you give yourself a foundation you can build on with confidence.

articles.title: Protect Your Brand: Trademarks, Copyrights, and Your Business's Intellectual Property
articles.body: You spend years building a name. Customers learn to trust it, recommend it, and look for it. Then one day another business shows up using a name or logo almost identical to yours, and you discover that the brand you assumed was yours may not legally be yours at all. For many small business owners, intellectual property is the most valuable thing they own and the thing they have done the least to protect. Understanding the basic tools, and using them before there is a problem, is one of the smartest moves you can make. The first thing to clear up is a common and costly misunderstanding. Registering your business name with the state, or forming an LLC, does not give you ownership of that name as a brand. It simply lets you operate under it in that state. Trademark protection is an entirely separate matter. A trademark protects the words, names, symbols, and logos that identify your goods or services and distinguish them from everyone else's. It is what stops a competitor from trading on the reputation you built by using a confusingly similar name. You actually gain some trademark rights just by using a name in commerce, but those rights are limited and local. Federal registration through the United States Patent and Trademark Office is what gives you far stronger, nationwide protection and a public record that the name is yours. That distinction matters most at the very beginning, before you commit to a name. The painful version of this story is the owner who builds a brand for years, only to receive a letter from a company that registered the same name first, demanding they stop using it. Doing a careful search before you settle on a name, and registering it once you do, is dramatically cheaper than rebranding an established business or fighting over who has the right to a name. A little homework at the start prevents a great deal of heartbreak later. Copyright protects something different: original creative work. The moment you create an original photograph, article, design, piece of music, video, or body of written content, copyright protection generally exists automatically. But registering your copyright with the Copyright Office strengthens your position significantly, giving you a clear record of ownership and access to stronger remedies if someone copies your work. For businesses whose value lives in the content they produce, that extra step is well worth taking. There is also a category of protection that does not come from any government office at all: the trade secret. A trade secret is valuable business information that gives you an edge precisely because competitors do not know it, from a recipe to a customer list to a process you have refined over years. Unlike trademarks and copyrights, you protect trade secrets not by registering them but by keeping them secret, which means using confidentiality agreements, limiting access, and being deliberate about who sees what. The protection lasts only as long as the secret does. Woven through all of these is one practical thread that owners overlook constantly: make sure you actually own the work you paid for. When you hire a freelancer to design your logo or build your website, the law does not automatically hand you the rights to what they create. Without a written agreement assigning ownership to you, the designer may still hold the copyright to the very logo your business is built around. A simple clause in your contracts, making clear that the work belongs to your business, closes a gap that surprises owners far too often. Protecting your brand is not a single task but a habit of foresight: search before you name, register what matters, document who owns what, and guard your secrets. Each step is modest on its own, and together they keep the most valuable, least replaceable parts of your business firmly in your hands. Because these questions are easy to get wrong and expensive to fix after the fact, they are exactly the kind of thing worth running past a professional early. articles.title: The Clauses Every Small Business Contract Should Include
articles.body: Most disputes between businesses do not happen because someone was dishonest. They happen because two people remembered the same conversation differently, and there was nothing in writing to settle the question. A good contract is not a sign of distrust; it is a shared memory that everyone agreed to in advance. And the strength of that memory depends almost entirely on which clauses you bothered to include. A handful of well-chosen provisions can save you from the most common and costly fights a small business ever has. Start with the foundation of any agreement: a clear description of the work. The scope of work, sometimes called the statement of work, spells out exactly what is being delivered, by whom, and to what standard. Vagueness here is where projects go to die. When a contract says a vendor will build a website but never defines how many pages, what features, or when it is finished, you have planted the seed of an argument. A precise scope protects both sides, because it tells everyone what counts as done, and just as importantly, what would be an extra you can charge for. Next comes the clause owners care about most once money is tight: payment terms. A strong agreement does not just name a price; it says when payment is due, in what amounts, by what method, and what happens if it is late. Will you require a deposit before starting? Will you bill in milestones? Do unpaid invoices accrue a late fee after a set number of days? Spelling this out does more than secure your cash flow. It quietly signals that you are a professional who expects to be paid on time, and it gives you something concrete to point to when a client drifts past the due date. The third essential is the one people least like to think about: how the relationship ends. A termination clause explains how either party can walk away, how much notice is required, and what is owed when they do. Without it, you can find yourself trapped in a deal that no longer makes sense, or blindsided when the other side vanishes. A good termination clause is like knowing where the exits are before the movie starts; you hope you never need them, but you are grateful they are clearly marked. Closely related is a clause that decides what happens when there is a genuine disagreement. A dispute resolution provision can require the parties to attempt mediation or to use arbitration before anyone races to court, and it can specify which state's laws govern the agreement and where any dispute will be heard. These details feel like fine print until the day they become the whole ballgame. Deciding the rules of engagement while everyone is calm is far cheaper than fighting about the rules themselves later. Beyond these, a few more provisions earn their place in most agreements. A confidentiality clause protects sensitive information you share while working together. A clause clarifying ownership of work product decides who owns what gets created, which matters enormously for anything involving designs, code, or content. A limitation of liability clause caps how much you could owe if something goes wrong, keeping a small mistake from turning into a business-ending judgment. And a force majeure clause addresses what happens when events truly outside anyone's control disrupt the deal. The theme running through all of these is simple: a contract earns its keep in the hard moments, not the easy ones. When everyone is happy and the work is going well, the document sits in a drawer. It only matters when something breaks, and at that point you cannot add the clause you wish you had written. That is why the time to think carefully about your agreements is before you sign them, and ideally with a professional who has seen how these disputes actually unfold. Reviewing contracts before you sign, and making sure your own agreements include the protections that matter, is exactly the kind of routine legal work most owners skip because hiring a lawyer by the hour feels expensive. Having affordable, ongoing access to counsel who can review and strengthen your contracts as a normal part of doing business is precisely what a small business plan through LegalShield is designed to provide. To learn more: click HERE. Put the right clauses in place while everyone is still smiling, and you turn your contracts from wishful paperwork into real protection.

articles.title: Non-Competes and NDAs: What Actually Protects Your Business
articles.body: Sooner or later, most business owners share something valuable with someone else. You bring on an employee who learns your methods, hire a contractor who sees your customer list, or talk with a potential partner about an idea you have not yet launched. In those moments, the instinct to protect what you have built is healthy. Two tools come up again and again in that conversation, the non-disclosure agreement and the non-compete agreement, and they are among the most misunderstood instruments in business. Knowing what each actually does, and what genuinely holds up, lets you protect your business without leaning on false confidence. Start with the more straightforward and more broadly useful of the two: the non-disclosure agreement, often called an NDA or confidentiality agreement. An NDA is a promise that the information you share will be kept confidential and not used or revealed without permission. It is the right tool whenever you need to hand sensitive information to someone, whether an employee, a vendor, a freelancer, or a prospective partner, while keeping the right to control where it goes. Because it protects information rather than restricting a person's ability to earn a living, an NDA is generally far easier to enforce than a non-compete, and it belongs in the toolkit of nearly every business that has secrets worth keeping. The non-compete agreement is a very different animal. It attempts to restrict someone, usually a former employee or a seller of a business, from competing with you for a period of time within a certain area. The appeal is obvious: you do not want to train someone only to watch them walk across the street and use everything they learned against you. But this is precisely where owners place too much faith in a signed page, because non-competes are far harder to enforce than people assume, and the rules around them have been shifting significantly. Whether a non-compete will hold up depends heavily on where you are and on the specifics of the agreement, and in a growing number of places the restrictions are limited, disfavored, or in certain contexts unenforceable altogether. Even where non-competes are allowed, courts tend to look at them skeptically and enforce only what is reasonable. An agreement that is too broad, lasting too long, covering too wide an area, or sweeping in more than is genuinely necessary to protect the business, can be narrowed or thrown out entirely. The lesson is not that non-competes are worthless, but that an overreaching one offers a false sense of security. A narrowly tailored agreement that protects a legitimate interest stands a far better chance than an aggressive one that tries to lock a person out of their entire profession. This is why, for many owners, the most reliable protection comes not from trying to stop people from competing but from protecting the specific things that give the business its edge. Well-drafted confidentiality agreements guard your sensitive information. Narrower agreements, such as one preventing a former employee from soliciting your existing customers or staff, are often more enforceable and more directly useful than a sweeping ban on competition. And treating your most valuable information as a genuine trade secret, by limiting who can access it and being deliberate about how it is handled, protects it in a way no signature alone can. The through line is that these agreements are only as good as how carefully they are written and how well they fit the law where you operate. A form pulled off the internet may be too weak to help or too broad to hold, and either way it can leave you exposed at the very moment you were counting on it. Because enforceability turns on details and on rules that differ from place to place and continue to evolve, this is an area where generic templates are especially risky and tailored guidance is especially valuable. Getting these agreements right, so they actually protect your business instead of merely looking protective, is exactly the kind of work that rewards professional input. articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: One Account Away from Trouble: Why You Must Separate Business and Personal Finances
articles.body: Many owners do the hard, smart work of forming an LLC or corporation to put a wall between their business and their personal life, and then quietly knock a hole in that wall without ever noticing. They pay a business expense from their personal account, cover a personal bill from the business account, and run everything through one comfortable, convenient pile of money. It feels harmless. It is not. Mixing business and personal finances is one of the most common ways owners undermine the very protection they paid to create. To see why, remember what an LLC or corporation actually does. It creates a separate legal person, distinct from you, so that if the business is sued or cannot pay its debts, your personal home, savings, and property are generally shielded. But that shield depends on the business genuinely being separate. When your finances are tangled together, you hand anyone suing your business a powerful argument: that the company was never really a separate entity at all, just you under a different name. Courts have a phrase for what can happen next, piercing the corporate veil, and it means a judge sets aside your liability protection and lets creditors reach your personal assets. The single most cited reason for it is commingling funds. The fix begins with something simple and almost embarrassingly basic: a dedicated business bank account. Every dollar the business earns goes into it, and every business expense comes out of it. This one habit does more to preserve your liability protection than almost anything else, because it draws a clear, visible line between the company's money and yours. Pairing it with a business credit or debit card used only for business purchases reinforces that line every single day. When the accounts are separate, the story your records tell is unmistakable: this is a real business, run like one. Keeping the money separate also transforms something owners dread, which is taxes. When business and personal expenses share an account, every tax season becomes an archaeological dig through hundreds of transactions, trying to remember which dinner was a client meeting and which was just dinner. Clean, separate books make it far easier to claim every legitimate deduction, to hand your accountant something usable, and to survive scrutiny if a tax authority ever comes asking. Disorganized, blended records do the opposite, costing you both deductions and credibility. There is a right way to move money between yourself and your business, and it is not by swiping the business card at the grocery store. When you want to take money out, you pay yourself deliberately, whether as an owner's draw or a salary, in a documented transfer from the business account to your personal one. When you need to put money in, you record it as a contribution or a loan to the business. The mechanics are simple; the discipline is the point. Every clean, recorded transfer reinforces that the business and the owner are two separate parties dealing with each other at arm's length, which is exactly the impression your protection depends on. None of this requires sophisticated accounting software or a finance background. It requires a decision to treat your business as the separate thing you went to the trouble of creating. Open the separate account, run business money through it and only it, pay yourself on purpose, and keep your records clean. These are small, unglamorous habits, but they are the difference between a liability shield that holds and one that collapses the first time it is tested. If you are not sure whether you are keeping things separate well enough, or you suspect you have been blurring the line, that is a question worth resolving before a problem ever arises, when fixing it is easy. Having affordable, ongoing access to legal counsel who can review how your business is structured and operated, and help you keep your protection intact, is exactly what a small business plan through LegalShield is designed to provide. articles.excerpt: You formed an LLC to protect your personal assets, but one common habit can quietly undo that protection: mixing business and personal money. Here is why keeping them separate is one of the most important things you can do.
