articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: Getting Paid: How to Protect Your Business from Late and Non-Paying Clients
articles.body: There is a particular kind of stress that only business owners know: you did the work, you did it well, and weeks later you are still staring at an unpaid invoice. Late and non-paying clients are not just an annoyance. For a small business, cash flow is oxygen, and a few slow payers can put real strain on payroll, rent, and your own peace of mind. The good news is that getting paid is not mostly about chasing money after the fact. It is about building protection into the relationship from the very beginning, long before an invoice is ever late. The single most powerful tool is the one owners most often skip out of politeness: a clear written agreement before the work starts. When the terms of payment are spelled out and signed, you transform a vague expectation into an enforceable commitment. The agreement should state the total price, when payments are due, what form they take, and what happens if they are late. Clients rarely argue with terms they agreed to in writing. It is the unwritten deal, built on a friendly assumption, that turns into a standoff. One of the most effective protections is to simply not extend all your trust at once. Requiring a deposit before you begin, and structuring larger projects into milestone payments, means you are never carrying the full cost of the work while hoping to be paid at the end. A deposit also filters out the least serious clients before you have invested your time, because someone unwilling to put any money down up front is often the same someone who will be hard to collect from later. Getting paid as you go keeps your exposure small at every stage. Your invoices themselves are a quiet line of defense. A professional invoice that clearly states the amount, the due date, accepted payment methods, and any late fee leaves no room for confusion or convenient forgetfulness. Sending invoices promptly matters more than people realize, because the longer you wait to ask, the longer the client feels free to wait to pay. A clear due date and an easy way to pay remove the small frictions that let payment slip down someone's to-do list. When a payment does go past due, the way you respond makes a real difference. Start early and stay professional. A friendly reminder the day after the due date is not pushy; it is simply good business, and it often resolves the matter, since many late payments are honest oversights rather than refusals. If reminders go unanswered, your tone can firm up gradually, moving from a gentle nudge to a formal written notice that references the agreement and states clearly what is owed and by when. Keeping a calm, documented paper trail protects you and frequently prompts payment without any further steps. If an account remains unpaid despite your efforts, you still have options, and knowing they exist changes how confidently you can operate. A formal demand letter, especially one on legal letterhead, signals that you are serious and willing to pursue the matter, and it often shakes loose a payment that reminders could not. Beyond that, small claims court offers an accessible path for many ordinary unpaid bills without the cost of a full lawsuit. The point is not to rush to confrontation but to know that you are not helpless, which lets you negotiate from a position of strength rather than hope. Underneath all of these tactics is a mindset worth adopting: protecting your right to be paid is not greedy or distrustful. It is what keeps your business alive so you can keep serving the clients who do value your work. Clear agreements, deposits, prompt professional invoices, and a calm escalation plan together turn getting paid from a recurring worry into a routine part of running your business. Much of this comes down to having the right paperwork in place and knowing what to do when a client crosses the line, which is exactly where many owners feel out of their depth. Having affordable, ongoing access to legal counsel who can help you set up solid payment agreements and send a demand letter when one is needed is precisely what a small business plan through LegalShield is built to provide. To learn more: click HERE. Build protection into how you get paid, and you protect the lifeblood of your business.
articles.excerpt: Doing the work is only half the job. Getting paid for it is the other half, and the one that quietly sinks more small businesses. Here is how to protect your cash flow before, during, and after the invoice goes out.
articles.seo_keywords: getting paid, late paying clients, non-paying clients, invoice protection, small business cash flow, collecting payment, deposits, protect your business

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: The Handshake Deal Trap: Why Every Business Agreement Belongs in Writing
articles.body: There is something deeply appealing about a handshake deal. It feels honorable and human, a sign that two people trust each other enough to skip the lawyers and the paperwork. For generations of business owners it has been a point of pride. And yet a striking number of the worst, most relationship-ending disputes a small business ever faces begin with exactly that handshake. The problem is not that the people were dishonest. The problem is that memory is unreliable, circumstances change, and a deal that lived only in two heads has no way to settle the question when those heads remember it differently. Consider how an honest disagreement is born. Two people shake hands on a project. One walks away believing the price covered everything; the other believing it covered only the first phase. Both are sincere. Both genuinely remember the conversation their own way. Months later, when the work expands and the bill grows, there is no document to consult, only two confident and conflicting memories. What started as a friendly arrangement curdles into resentment, not because anyone lied, but because nothing was written down to anchor the truth. A written agreement is simply that anchor. It helps to understand that a verbal agreement is often a real, legally binding contract. The myth that a deal does not count unless it is on paper is just that, a myth, in many situations. The trouble is not that oral agreements lack force; it is that they lack proof. If a verbal deal falls apart and ends up in front of a judge, the entire case can come down to your word against theirs, with little evidence to tip the balance. You may be completely in the right and still lose, simply because you cannot prove what was agreed. Writing it down does not create the obligation so much as preserve the evidence of it. There is also a category of agreements that generally must be in writing to be enforceable at all. While the details vary, the law in most places requires written contracts for certain things, such as deals that cannot be completed within a year, agreements involving the sale of real estate, and promises to cover someone else's debt. An owner who relies on a handshake for one of these can discover too late that the agreement is not enforceable in the way they assumed. This is one more reason that, when something important is on the line, a written record is not optional but essential. Putting a deal in writing does not require a thick contract drafted in dense legal language. Even a short, clear document, or a confirming email that lays out what was agreed, who does what, by when, and for how much, is dramatically better than nothing. The act of writing it down has a quiet secondary benefit, too: it forces both sides to discover the gaps in their understanding before the work begins, while there is still time to talk them through. Many disputes are prevented not by the document itself but by the conversation that creating it requires. Perhaps the most important reframe is this: getting it in writing is not an act of distrust toward the other person. It is an act of respect for the relationship. The clearest, most durable partnerships are the ones where both sides know exactly what was promised, so that no honest mistake or fading memory can ever turn into a feud. Writing it down protects not just your money but the goodwill between you, which is often worth far more. The practical obstacle, of course, is that owners rarely feel equipped to put agreements into proper writing, and hiring a lawyer for every handshake feels absurd. That is exactly the gap worth closing. Having affordable, ongoing access to legal counsel who can help you put your agreements in writing and review the ones handed to you is precisely what a small business plan through LegalShield is designed to provide. To learn more: click HERE. Shake hands if you like, but write it down too, and you protect both your business and the relationships that make it possible.
articles.excerpt: A handshake feels honorable, trusting, and simple. It is also where some of the most painful business disputes begin. Here is why putting it in writing protects the relationship as much as the money.
articles.seo_keywords: handshake deal, verbal agreement, get it in writing, business agreement, written contract, oral contract, small business disputes, protect your business

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: Council Bluff: When Lewis and Clark Met the Oto and Missouria Near Omaha
articles.body: More than two centuries ago, long before Omaha's bridges and skyline, a small band of explorers worked their way up the Missouri River and paused on a windswept bluff just north of where our city now stands. There, in the first days of August 1804, the Corps of Discovery led by Captains Meriwether Lewis and William Clark held their very first formal council with Native American leaders. They named the spot "Council Bluff," and with that meeting, the Omaha area earned a lasting place in the story of America's great westward expedition. The journey to our doorstep had begun months earlier. President Thomas Jefferson had charged Lewis and Clark with exploring the vast lands of the Louisiana Purchase, finding a route toward the Pacific, and building friendships with the many Native nations who had lived on these lands for generations. The Corps set out from Camp Dubois near St. Louis on May 14, 1804, pushing a fifty-five-foot keelboat and two smaller pirogues against the muddy current. Theirs was slow, grueling work. Day after day the men rowed, poled, and sometimes hauled their boats upstream by rope, often covering only a dozen or so miles between dawn and dusk. By late July they had reached the mouth of the Platte River near present-day Omaha, a landmark that rivermen treated as a gateway to the upper Missouri. Hoping to meet the Native peoples of the region, the captains sent word inviting local leaders to a council. On the evening of August 2, a delegation of Oto and Missouria men arrived at the expedition's camp. The next morning, August 3, 1804, the two groups gathered on the bluff for a day of speeches, gifts, and ceremony. Lewis and Clark spoke of the new American government and their hopes for peace and trade. They distributed peace medals and presents, smoked a pipe together, and demonstrated curiosities that drew genuine wonder, including an air gun, a compass, a magnet, and a spyglass. It was a respectful, hopeful first encounter, and it set the pattern for dozens of councils the Corps would hold across the continent in the months and years to come. The Oto and Missouria were the gracious hosts of this meeting, and their presence reminds us that the land around Omaha was a gathering place and a homeland long before the expedition arrived. These two related nations, who would later join together as the Otoe-Missouria, had deep roots along the Missouri and Platte rivers. The council was not the beginning of the region's history but a single chapter in a much longer Native story, one worth remembering with gratitude and respect for the peoples who first welcomed these travelers. It is worth clearing up a friendly bit of confusion that has lingered ever since. The "Council Bluff" that Lewis and Clark named sits on the Nebraska side of the river, roughly twenty miles north of downtown Omaha near the modern town of Fort Calhoun. It is not the same place as the city of Council Bluffs, Iowa, which lies downstream on the opposite bank. That Iowa city, first settled as Kanesville, borrowed the name only in 1852, drawing on the fame of the original bluff to the north. So when you hear "Council Bluff," picture the Nebraska heights where the expedition actually camped, not the busy Iowa city across the water. The captains were so impressed with the high, commanding ground that they recommended it as the site for a future government post. Years later, in 1820, the United States Army acted on that very suggestion and built Fort Atkinson there, the first U.S. military post west of the Missouri River. At its peak the fort housed as many as twelve hundred soldiers and played an important role in the fur trade and in relations with Native nations until it closed in 1827. Today a faithfully recreated Fort Atkinson welcomes families to walk its grounds and imagine those early days, a tangible link back to the council that gave the place its name. The story of Council Bluff is a proud thread in the larger tapestry of how this region came to be, connecting to the chapters of how Omaha began and the later winter quarters at Florence. When you stand on the bluffs above the Missouri and watch the river roll by, you are looking at the same waters that carried the Corps of Discovery toward the unknown, and at the very ground where Omaha's heritage and the nation's grand adventure first met.
articles.excerpt: In the summer of 1804, the Corps of Discovery paddled and pulled their way up the Missouri River past the future site of Omaha and paused for a landmark moment. On a high bluff they named Council Bluff, Lewis and Clark held their first formal council with Native American leaders, tying our region forever to one of America's greatest journeys.
articles.seo_keywords: Lewis and Clark Omaha, Council Bluff Nebraska, Oto Missouria council 1804, Corps of Discovery Missouri River, Fort Atkinson Fort Calhoun, first council Lewis and Clark, Nebraska history, Missouri River expedition

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: The Dreamland Ballroom: Where North Omaha Danced Through the Jazz Age
articles.body: Climb the stairs of the Jewell Building on North 24th Street, and you step into one of the most joyful chapters in Omaha's story. Built in 1923 by businessman James "Jimmy" Jewell Sr., who was widely regarded as one of the most successful Black entrepreneurs in the city, this handsome brick building gave North Omaha something it had been waiting for. On its second floor, behind tall windows overlooking the bustle of the street below, Jewell opened the Dreamland Ballroom, a spacious dance hall that would become the beating heart of the Near North Side for more than forty years. The Dreamland was not simply a place to hear music. It was where a whole community gathered to celebrate, to dress in its finest, and to share in the irresistible sound that defined an era. The timing could not have been better. In the 1920s, Omaha sat at a crossroads of the nation's great rail lines, and the city quickly became a favorite stop along the touring circuit that carried musicians between Chicago, Kansas City, Denver, and Minneapolis. Bands rolling through the heartland needed a hall large enough and welcoming enough to play, and the Dreamland fit the bill perfectly. Jewell, and later his son Jimmy Jewell Jr., who carried on the family business with a showman's instinct, made it their mission to bring the very best to North 24th Street. They succeeded beyond measure. Over the decades, the Dreamland's stage welcomed a procession of giants whose names still ring through music history. To list those who played there is to recite a roll call of American legends. Duke Ellington brought his orchestra. So did Count Basie, whose connection to Omaha ran deep, and Louis Armstrong, whose trumpet could fill a room with pure delight. Dizzy Gillespie and Lionel Hampton appeared, as did Nat "King" Cole, Sarah Vaughan, Dinah Washington, Billy Eckstine, Ray Charles, Louis Jordan, and Fats Domino. For dancers crowding the floor on a warm evening, the thrill of standing a few feet from such artistry must have felt like a dream come to life, which makes the ballroom's name seem all the more fitting. Yet the Dreamland was never only a destination for touring stars. It was also a nursery for Omaha's own remarkable talent, and this may be the part of the story that matters most. The city's vibrant scene produced homegrown musicians who went on to national acclaim. Saxophonist and bandleader Preston Love, born and raised in Omaha, got his start in local orchestras before joining Count Basie's band and later working with Ray Charles. Anna Mae Winburn led groups in the neighborhood before becoming one of the most celebrated leaders of the all-women orchestra known as the International Sweethearts of Rhythm. In 1936, the gifted bandleader Nat Towles settled his orchestra into a residency at the Dreamland and reshaped the city's sound, training young players who would scatter across the country and carry Omaha's musical reputation with them. These were the territory bands, hardworking ensembles managed and booked right out of Omaha, that crisscrossed the Midwest and helped make the region a true incubator of jazz. All of this flourished within a neighborhood that was rich in spirit and ambition. North 24th Street, the main artery of the Near North Side, was the cultural and commercial center of Omaha's African American community, lined with shops, restaurants, theaters, and gathering places. Music spilled from its doorways, and on a given night the avenue hummed with energy. The Dreamland anchored that world, offering a place where families and friends could come together in celebration. For readers who love the city's wider story of stages and performers, the Dreamland stands alongside grand venues like the historic Omaha Orpheum Theatre, and it shaped many of the notable figures who called Omaha home. The Dreamland Ballroom eventually fell quiet, its dancing days giving way to changing times, but the Jewell Building still stands as a treasured landmark, listed on the National Register of Historic Places and honored as one of the first major commercial buildings built by and for Omaha's Black community. To walk past it today is to feel the echo of trumpets and laughter, of polished shoes gliding across a wooden floor, of a neighborhood that gave the world far more music than its size might suggest. The Dreamland reminds us that greatness often grows in close-knit places, and that Omaha, perched on the prairie, once swung with the very best of the jazz age.
articles.excerpt: On the second floor of a handsome brick building on North 24th Street, the Dreamland Ballroom turned Omaha into a beloved stop on the great jazz touring circuit. For more than forty years, the biggest names in American music climbed those stairs, and so did the neighborhood's own homegrown legends.
articles.seo_keywords: Dreamland Ballroom, Jewell Building, North Omaha jazz, North 24th Street, James Jewell, Near North Side, Omaha music history, territory bands

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: The Umonhon: The Upstream People Who Named Omaha
articles.body: Every day, people drive the highways and cross the bridges of Omaha, Nebraska, rarely pausing to wonder where that musical name first came from. The answer reaches back centuries and far up the Missouri River, to a people who still carry the name as their own: the Umonhon, known in English as the Omaha. They are not a footnote to the city's history. They are its first chapter, and a living one. To learn their story is to discover that the word Omaha is itself a quiet testament to courage, movement, and an enduring bond with the land. In their own Siouan language, the people call themselves Umonhon, a name most often translated as "those going against the wind" or "those going against the current," and more simply as the "upstream people." That meaning is no accident of language; it is a memory of a journey. Tribal tradition and historians alike trace the Omaha's ancestral roots to the lower Ohio Valley, from which their forebears gradually moved westward and northward. Along the way they parted from related peoples such as the Ponca, Osage, Kansa, and Quapaw, and made their way up the Missouri River into what is now northeastern Nebraska, western Iowa, and the surrounding plains. To go against the current was both a literal direction of travel and a fitting description of a determined people. You can sense that same spirit in the way their old earth lodges were built, with the doorway always facing east toward the rising sun, a daily reminder of where they had come from and the long road upstream that had brought them home. Theirs was a rich and ordered way of life. The Omaha organized themselves into two great halves, the Sky People and the Earth People, each made up of clans with their own duties; the Sky People tended to the tribe's spiritual life, while the Earth People cared for its physical welfare. For much of the year they lived in sturdy, dome-shaped earth lodges in settled villages, farming corn, beans, and squash in the river bottoms. Twice a year they left to hunt bison across the open plains, living in hide tepees on the move. At the heart of their spiritual life stood the Sacred Pole, a cottonwood figure called Umonhonti, "The Real Omaha," revered as a living person and kept in a sacred tent at the center of the village. It united the people as one community and embodied their shared identity, a presence as real to them as kinship itself. The Omaha have long honored thoughtful leaders. Among the most remembered is Big Elk, a respected principal chief who guided his people through an era of rapid change as settlers pressed westward. His grandson by his daughter, Logan Fontenelle, the son of a French-American fur trader, grew up fluent in both Omaha and English and served as an interpreter in the tribe's dealings with the United States government. In the treaty negotiations of the 1850s, Fontenelle played a central role, and in the Treaty of 1854 the Omaha ceded vast lands while reserving a homeland in present-day Thurston County. His life was cut short in 1855 during a summer buffalo hunt, but his name endures across Nebraska and Iowa in places, parks, and the wooded sanctuary of Fontenelle Forest near the city. It was on Omaha land, ceded under that treaty, that speculators from across the river founded the new city in 1854 and gave it the tribe's name. The bluffs where downtown now stands, the river the city grew along, the very ground beneath its neighborhoods, all of it was long the homeland of the upstream people. Understanding that connection adds depth to any visit and pairs naturally with the larger story of how Omaha began. The Omaha story did not end with treaties or a founding date. The Omaha Tribe of Nebraska remains a sovereign nation today, with its homeland on the Omaha Reservation and its tribal headquarters in the community of Macy, in the northeastern corner of the state. There, descendants of Big Elk and his people continue to speak their language, hold their annual celebrations, and pass their heritage to new generations. The next time you say the name of the city, remember that it is a gift from a living people, the upstream people who went against the wind, and who are still here.
articles.excerpt: Long before a city rose along the Missouri, the Umonhon called these bluffs home. Their name means those who go against the wind, and their story of migration, leadership, and survival is woven into the very name of Omaha. Meet the people whose heritage still lives on at Macy, Nebraska.
articles.seo_keywords: Omaha Tribe, Umonhon, Omaha name meaning, Logan Fontenelle, Omaha Reservation, Macy Nebraska, Big Elk, Omaha history, Nebraska Native American, upstream people

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: Born in Omaha: The North Omaha Roots of Malcolm X
articles.body: Among the many notable figures who can trace their first chapter to Omaha, few left a mark on American history as profound as the man the world came to know as Malcolm X. He was born here on May 19, 1925, in a small house in North Omaha, and though his family moved away while he was still a toddler, the city remains the place where his life story began. For visitors and residents alike, his Omaha roots are a meaningful part of the community's broader heritage and a reminder that history often takes its first breath in ordinary neighborhoods. When he was born, he was named Malcolm Little, the fourth of his parents' children. His father, the Reverend Earl Little, was a Baptist preacher from Georgia, and his mother, Louise Little, was a seamstress and writer originally from Grenada. Both were active supporters of Marcus Garvey's Universal Negro Improvement Association, a movement that promoted Black pride, self-reliance, and community organizing across the country. Louise contributed writing to the movement's efforts, and Earl helped organize local chapters. Their home life in Omaha was shaped by faith, family, and a deep commitment to bettering the lives of those around them. The Little family lived at 3448 Pinkney Street, in the heart of North Omaha, a neighborhood that has long stood at the center of the city's African American community and cultural life. The family's time in Omaha was brief. By around 1926, when Malcolm was still an infant, they relocated, eventually settling in the Midwest as Earl's work and the family's circumstances led them onward. Malcolm would spend his childhood elsewhere, but the address on Pinkney Street endured as the starting point of a life that would go on to influence generations of Americans. In the decades that followed, Malcolm grew into one of the most prominent and widely discussed public figures of the twentieth century, known for his powerful oratory and his lifelong dedication to the dignity and advancement of Black Americans. He traveled, studied, and spoke before audiences around the world, and his autobiography became a landmark of American letters that is still read in classrooms today. Through all of that journey, the simple fact remained that the man whose words reached millions had taken his very first breath in Omaha, Nebraska. He joins a long roster of remarkable individuals with ties to the city, a heritage you can explore further among Omaha's famous people. Today, Omaha honors that connection through the work of the Malcolm X Memorial Foundation, established in 1971 by Omaha resident Rowena Moore. Moore devoted herself to preserving the birthsite and ensuring that her city's link to this historic figure would not be forgotten. Largely through her persistence, the location was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1984, and a historical marker was later placed to identify the grounds. Over the years, the Foundation gathered roughly seventeen acres of land surrounding the original home site, transforming a quiet lot into a place of remembrance, education, and community gathering. The original house no longer stands, but the open grounds, the marker, and the visitor facilities invite reflection on the family who once lived there. The birthsite has become a gathering place where students, families, and travelers come to learn, to commemorate, and to consider the long arc of American history that began on this North Omaha block. In that respect, it takes its place alongside other sites where Nebraska history was made and remembered, much as the legacy preserved in the story of the Standing Bear trial continues to teach visitors about courage and dignity on the plains. To stand on Pinkney Street is to be reminded that great lives often begin in humble surroundings, and that a single neighborhood can hold a thread connecting a local community to the wider story of a nation. Malcolm X's path led far from the city of his birth, yet Omaha proudly claims him as one of its own. Through the careful stewardship of those who built and maintain his memorial, the place where Malcolm Little entered the world remains a quiet, dignified landmark, welcoming each new generation to remember where one extraordinary American journey first began.
articles.excerpt: Long before he became one of the most recognized voices in American history, Malcolm X entered the world as a baby named Malcolm Little in a modest house on Pinkney Street in North Omaha. This is the story of his Omaha beginnings and how the city remembers its remarkable native son today.
articles.seo_keywords: Malcolm X Omaha, Malcolm X birthsite, Malcolm Little, North Omaha history, Pinkney Street, Malcolm X Memorial Foundation, Omaha famous people, Nebraska history

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: The Omaha Star and Mildred Brown: A Light on North 24th Street
articles.body: On a summer day in 1938, a young woman with boundless energy and a white carnation often pinned to her lapel set out to give North Omaha something it sorely needed: a newspaper that would tell the fuller, truer story of its people. That woman was Mildred Brown, and the paper she helped bring to life, the Omaha Star, first reached readers on July 9, 1938. More than eight decades later, it still arrives each week, making it one of the most remarkable chapters in the history of this proud Nebraska city and a lasting testament to the vision of a publisher who refused to be told what a Black woman could and could not do. Brown founded the Star alongside her husband, Shirl Edward Gilbert, often known as S. Edward Gilbert, opening the paper's doors at 2216 North 24th Street. That first edition ran five thousand copies and sold for a dime apiece. From the beginning, the Star carried an editorial spirit centered on "joy and happiness," choosing to spotlight the achievements, families, churches, and everyday triumphs of African American life at a time when the wider press too often ignored them or printed only the worst. In an era of segregation, deciding to celebrate a community was itself a quietly radical act, and readers responded by making the Star their own. When Brown and Gilbert divorced in 1943, Mildred took back her maiden name and full control of the enterprise, becoming founder, publisher, and editor all at once. Over the following decades she steered the paper through enormous change. What had begun as a source of uplifting neighborhood news grew, by the 1950s and 1960s, into a determined voice for civil rights. The Star reported on the struggles and victories of the movement, gave space to local activists, and held a mirror up to a city that did not always want to look. Brown's work earned national notice; President Lyndon B. Johnson commended her coverage during the turbulent 1960s, and she later traveled abroad as a goodwill ambassador. Through it all she kept her office, and for many years her home, at the back of the building on North 24th Street, the very street that served as the commercial and cultural spine of Black Omaha. Brown's place in history is genuinely pioneering, and it deserves to be stated with care. She is widely recognized as the founder of the longest-running Black newspaper in the United States established by an African American woman, a distinction that reflects both her trailblazing role and the extraordinary endurance of the paper she built. Running a weekly newspaper is hard, often thankless work, and doing so for fifty-one years, as Brown did until her death in 1989, required a rare blend of business sense, courage, and devotion to community. She belongs in any conversation about Omaha's most influential figures, a list explored further in our piece on famous people from Omaha. The Star's story did not end with its founder. After Mildred Brown passed away in 1989, her niece, Dr. Marguerita Washington, carried the paper forward, preserving both its mission and its remarkable streak of never missing a weekly edition. That continuity is part of what makes the Omaha Star so meaningful. It links the Omaha of the 1930s to the Omaha of today, threading together generations of readers who found in its pages proof that their lives mattered and their stories were worth telling. The newspaper's longtime home on North 24th Street has come to be cherished as a landmark of that heritage, a place where ink and ideals met week after week. To walk past that building, or simply to open a copy of the Star, is to feel the steady pulse of a community that has long fought to be seen on its own terms. Mildred Brown understood that a newspaper could be more than a record of events; it could be a gathering place, an advocate, and a source of pride. In that sense, the Omaha Star stands alongside other landmarks of Nebraska's long pursuit of dignity and justice, much as the courtroom drama recounted in our story of the Standing Bear trial did in an earlier age. The white carnation Mildred Brown so often wore has faded into memory, but the light she lit on North 24th Street still shines, a reminder that one person's determination can illuminate a city for generations to come.
articles.excerpt: In 1938, a determined young woman named Mildred Brown launched a newspaper that would outlast generations and become the heartbeat of North Omaha's Black community. Discover the story of the Omaha Star, its home on North 24th Street, and the trailblazing publisher who guided it with grace and grit.
articles.seo_keywords: Omaha Star newspaper, Mildred Brown, North Omaha history, North 24th Street, Black-owned newspaper, Omaha Nebraska heritage, African American newspapers, civil rights Omaha

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: A Gift in His Memory: The Founding of Creighton University
articles.body: Few stories capture the spirit of early Omaha as movingly as the founding of Creighton University, an institution born not of ambition but of love and remembrance. In 1878, a school opened its doors on a bluff above the young river city, established through the will of a grieving widow who wished to honor her late husband. That husband was Edward Creighton, a self-made pioneer of the American frontier, and the school created in his memory would grow into one of the great cornerstones of education and medicine in the region. Edward Creighton's life reads like the very story of the expanding nation. Born in 1820 on a farm in Belmont County, Ohio, into an Irish Catholic family, he had little formal schooling but an abundance of determination. As a young man he found his calling in the rising business of telegraphy, and by the 1850s he had become one of the foremost builders of telegraph lines in the country. In 1856 he married Mary Lucretia Wareham in Dayton, Ohio, and the couple soon made their home in Omaha, then a rough and hopeful settlement on the Missouri River. It was a city very much in the making, as readers can explore in our look at how Omaha began. Edward's greatest achievement came in the winter of 1860 and 1861, when he surveyed the route of the transcontinental telegraph and supervised construction of the line stretching westward from Omaha toward Salt Lake City. Completed in October of 1861, this line at last joined the Atlantic and Pacific coasts by wire, allowing a message to cross the continent in moments rather than weeks. The achievement helped knit a divided and distant nation together, and it made Edward Creighton a wealthy and respected man. He invested in Omaha with the same energy he brought to the wires, becoming the founding president of the First National Bank of Omaha in 1863 and a leader in ranching, freighting, and commerce. When he died in 1874, he left both a fortune and a deeply held wish that some of it serve the cause of education. That wish fell to his wife, Mary Lucretia. Though her own life was nearing its close, she remembered her husband's devotion to learning and his faith. In her will she set aside one hundred thousand dollars to establish a free Catholic college in his name. Mary Lucretia died in 1876, and after her passing the responsibility of carrying out her vision was taken up by the family. On the second of September in 1878, Creighton College welcomed its first students, roughly one hundred and twenty young people taught by five Jesuit priests and two lay instructors. True to Mary Lucretia's intent, tuition was free, opening the doors of learning to families of modest means in a way that was remarkable for its day. The college might have remained a small academy were it not for Edward's younger brother, John A. Creighton, and his wife Sarah Emily, who was herself one of Mary Lucretia's sisters. John was a prosperous merchant and rancher with the same generous instincts that ran through the family, and he devoted much of his fortune to nurturing the young school. Under his patronage the college expanded steadily, gaining new buildings and new ambitions. In 1892 the John A. Creighton Medical College was established, and the institution at last became a university in the fullest sense, joined to a hospital named in Sarah Emily's honor. Over the course of his life John gave roughly two million dollars to the school, and in 1895 Pope Leo XIII recognized his charitable works by naming him a Knight of the Holy Roman Empire. When John died in 1907, the modest college had grown into a true university of several schools. From those beginnings rose the Creighton University known today, a Jesuit and Catholic institution whose schools of medicine, dentistry, law, nursing, business, and the arts have educated generations and made Omaha a center of healing and scholarship. Its enduring motto and mission, rooted in service to others, echo the very generosity that brought it into being. Visitors strolling the historic streets of the Old Market are never far from a campus whose story began with a widow's tender act of remembrance. In honoring one man's memory, the Creighton family gave Omaha a gift that has never stopped giving, a living monument built not of stone alone but of faith, learning, and love.
articles.excerpt: When a grieving widow honored her late husband with a school, she set in motion one of Omaha's most enduring institutions. The story of Creighton University begins with a telegraph pioneer, a generous bequest, and a family whose faith and philanthropy helped shape a city.
articles.seo_keywords: Creighton University history, founding of Creighton University, Edward Creighton telegraph, Mary Lucretia Creighton, John A. Creighton, Omaha Jesuit university, Omaha heritage, Creighton College 1878

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: The Easter Sunday Tornado of 1913: Omaha's Day of Loss and the Resilience That Followed
articles.body: Of all the days that have shaped Omaha's story, few are remembered with as much tenderness and reverence as the evening of March 23, 1913. It was Easter Sunday, and the city had spent the day in its best clothes, visiting family, attending services, and savoring the first soft promise of spring. By nightfall, however, Omaha had been changed forever. A single, immense tornado swept diagonally across the city in a matter of minutes, and in its wake lay the deepest loss the community had ever known. More than a century later, the storm is remembered not only for the grief it brought, but for the quiet courage with which Omahans answered it. The day had given little warning. Witnesses recalled only a sudden, sharp drop in temperature and the darkening of the western sky before the tornado reached the city's edge at about a quarter to six in the evening. Rated in later years as an F4 on the Fujita scale, it grew as wide as a half mile in places and cut a path roughly four and a half miles long. It entered near Forty-second and Farnam and tore across some of Omaha's most cherished neighborhoods, sparing neither the grand homes along West Farnam Hill and the fashionable streets of the Gold Coast nor the modest cottages of working families. The storm pressed northward through Bemis Park and into the densely settled blocks of the Near North Side, and it touched the elegant district around Kountze Place before crossing toward the Missouri River. From the stately homes of the wealthy to the simple frame houses of laborers, the wind made no distinction, and that shared sorrow would soon become a shared resolve. The scale of the loss was staggering for a city of Omaha's size. Roughly one hundred lives were taken within the city and its immediate surroundings, with the broader outbreak claiming still more across Nebraska and into Iowa as earlier storms struck small towns like Yutan and Berlin. Hundreds more were injured. Some eight hundred homes were destroyed outright, and as many as two thousand others were damaged, leaving whole families without shelter on a cold spring night. Churches, schools, and beloved landmarks were reduced to splinters. For a young, growing city that had so recently celebrated its rise on the prairie, the sudden silence after the storm was almost unbearable. Yet what happened in the hours and days that followed is the part of the story Omaha has chosen to carry forward. Neighbors climbed over wreckage by lantern light to free those who were trapped. Doctors and nurses worked through the night, and ordinary citizens opened undamaged homes to families who had lost everything. Relief committees formed almost immediately, gathering food, clothing, and money, while aid arrived from across the region as word of Omaha's plight spread. The same instinct for community that had built the city, a story told in how Omaha began, now revealed itself in its truest form. People who had been strangers before that evening found themselves bound together by the simple work of caring for one another. Rebuilding came swiftly and stubbornly. Within days, the sound of hammers and saws filled streets that had so recently been quiet with mourning. Families cleared their lots, salvaged what timber they could, and began again. The disaster also taught the city hard lessons it would not forget, deepening Omaha's respect for the power of prairie weather and strengthening the civic habit of preparing for it. In time, sturdier homes rose where the wind had passed, and the rhythm of daily life, of markets and porches and Sunday gatherings of the kind still felt in places like the Omaha Old Market, returned to neighborhoods that had nearly been swept away. The Easter Sunday tornado remains the deadliest in Omaha's history, and it is right to remember those who were lost with dignity and gratitude. But the storm's truest legacy is not measured only in what was taken. It lives in the example set by a city that refused to let a single terrible night have the final word. Omaha mourned, then Omaha rebuilt, and in doing so it taught its children a lesson that has outlasted every storm since: that even in the face of great loss, a community that holds fast to one another can always begin again.
articles.excerpt: On Easter evening in 1913, the deadliest tornado in Omaha's history carved a path of sorrow across the city's neighborhoods. Yet from that night of loss rose a story of neighbors helping neighbors, of homes rebuilt, and of a community that refused to be defined by a single storm.
articles.seo_keywords: Omaha Easter tornado 1913, March 23 1913 tornado, deadliest Omaha tornado, Near North Side Omaha history, Field Club Omaha, Nebraska tornado history, Omaha heritage, West Farnam Hill

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: The Day the Tracks Began: How Omaha Launched the Transcontinental Railroad
articles.body: Every great journey has a starting point, and for one of the most ambitious undertakings in American history, that point was a bustling young river town on the western bank of the Missouri. Long before the first transcontinental railroad stitched the continent together, the dream had to begin somewhere with a single shovel of turned earth. That somewhere was Omaha, Nebraska, and the story of how this frontier community was chosen to launch the Union Pacific Railroad is woven deeply into the city's identity as the proud Gateway to the West. The choice was no accident. When President Abraham Lincoln signed the Pacific Railroad Act on July 1, 1862, he set in motion a plan for two companies to build toward one another and meet somewhere in the vast middle of the country. The Central Pacific would push eastward from Sacramento, California, while the Union Pacific would build westward from the eastern edge of the Missouri River at Council Bluffs, Iowa, directly across the water from Omaha. Much credit belongs to a determined young engineer named Grenville Dodge, who had surveyed the region and become convinced that the gentle, steady grade of the Platte River Valley offered the natural pathway across the plains. Summoned to confer with Lincoln, Dodge argued firmly that the line should follow that valley and begin at the Omaha and Council Bluffs crossing. On November 17, 1863, just two days before he delivered the Gettysburg Address, Lincoln issued an executive order fixing the eastern terminus exactly where Dodge had recommended. And so, on December 2, 1863, Omaha gathered to celebrate. At a ceremonial groundbreaking near the city's riverfront, residents turned the first earth for a railroad that would one day reach the Pacific. Cannons sounded, speeches rang out, and a festive celebration carried on at the Herndon House downtown. It was a moment of enormous optimism for a town only a decade old, and the people who lived there understood they were witnessing the beginning of something that would outlast them all. Those early dreamers had little idea of the obstacles ahead, yet their faith in the project never wavered. If you enjoy tracing the city's roots, you may also like reading about how Omaha began. The work that followed was slow at first, slowed by the Civil War and by the sheer challenge of supplying a railroad on the edge of settled country. The first rails were not laid until 1865, but once construction gained momentum it became a marvel of speed and human effort. Under Dodge, who was named the Union Pacific's chief engineer in 1866, crews of laborers, many of them veterans and immigrants, graded the roadbed and spiked down iron at a remarkable pace, pressing across Nebraska, Wyoming, and beyond. Mile by mile, the line that had started at Omaha climbed toward the mountains and the high desert, while the Central Pacific blasted and tunneled its way east through the Sierra Nevada. The two railroads finally met on May 10, 1869, at Promontory Summit in Utah Territory. There the locomotives Union Pacific No. 119 and Central Pacific's Jupiter were drawn nose to nose, and a ceremonial golden spike was driven to mark the joining of the rails. A telegraph carried a single triumphant word, "done," across the wires to a waiting nation. A journey that had once taken months by wagon could now be made in days, and the line that helped make it possible had begun its westward march from the banks of the Missouri at Omaha. What makes Omaha's role so enduring is what came after the celebration faded. The Union Pacific made the city its home and never left, and that steady presence shaped Omaha's destiny. The railroad brought workers, shops, freight, and commerce, drawing newcomers and feeding industries that turned the town into a major hub of trade and transportation. Generations later, the Union Pacific still anchors the city from its downtown headquarters, a living link to that December day in 1863. Visitors who wish to step back into the railroad era can explore the grand old depot now preserved as the Durham Museum in Union Station. To stand in Omaha today is to stand at a beginning. The rails that bound a continent together started here, carried west by the vision of engineers, the labor of thousands, and the hope of a young community that believed in what it could become. That is why Omaha earned its proud title as the Gateway to the West, and why the story of the transcontinental railroad will always be, at heart, an Omaha story.
articles.excerpt: When the first shovel of earth turned on a December afternoon in 1863, a young Nebraska town became the launching point for the greatest building project of its age. This is the story of how Omaha was chosen to start the Union Pacific Railroad and grew into America's Gateway to the West.
articles.seo_keywords: Omaha transcontinental railroad, Union Pacific Omaha, Omaha groundbreaking 1863, Gateway to the West, Grenville Dodge, Promontory Summit golden spike, Omaha history, Nebraska railroad heritage

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: The New White City: How Omaha's 1898 World's Fair Dazzled a Nation
articles.body: For five remarkable months in 1898, the prairie city of Omaha transformed itself into one of the most beautiful places in America. From June 1 to November 1, the Trans-Mississippi and International Exposition rose from the open land of North Omaha as a dazzling temporary metropolis, and for a generation of Nebraskans it became the defining event of their lives. The fair was conceived in late 1895 by a small committee of Omaha businessmen led by the banker Gurdon Wattles, who believed the region needed a grand stage to show the nation what the American West had become. What they built exceeded even their own ambitions, drawing more than 2.6 million visitors and announcing to the country that Omaha had arrived. The exposition occupied roughly 184 acres in the Kountze Place neighborhood, on ground that today includes Kountze Park near Twentieth and Pinkney streets. Land for the venture was made possible in part by the Omaha banker Herman Kountze, and the city that took shape there was unlike anything Nebraskans had seen. At its heart lay the Grand Court, a long formal basin of water stretching some 2,000 feet, encircled by about twenty-one stately buildings finished in brilliant white. Their classical domes, columns, and arches gleamed so brightly that the assembly earned the affectionate nickname the "New White City," an echo of the famous Chicago World's Fair of 1893. The palaces were never meant to last; they were built of temporary materials over wood frames. Yet by day they shone against the Nebraska sky, and by night thousands of electric lights traced their outlines and reflected in the still water of the lagoon, a spectacle of modern marvel to people who in many cases had grown up by lamplight. The fair's purpose was to celebrate the development of the entire territory west of the Mississippi River, all the way to the Pacific. Inside the great halls, visitors wandered among more than four thousand exhibits showcasing agriculture, mining, manufacturing, fine art, and the newest machines of the age. Families arrived by train from across the plains, strolling the Grand Court, riding the attractions, and marveling at displays gathered from across the country and beyond. It was an education, an entertainment, and a point of civic pride all at once, the kind of communal wonder that a growing city like the one chronicled in how Omaha began could scarcely have imagined a few decades earlier. Two moments lifted the exposition onto the national stage. The first was the Indian Congress, which formally opened on August 4 and became the largest gathering of Native nations of its era. More than five hundred Native Americans representing dozens of tribes and nations came to Omaha, among them many members of the Omaha and Winnebago peoples. Conceived as an ethnological exhibition, the Congress reflected the attitudes of its time, yet it also produced an extraordinary visual record. The official photographer, Frank A. Rinehart, made a celebrated series of portraits that survive today as a dignified and historically priceless gallery of faces and names from across Native America. The second great moment came on October 12, remembered as President's Day, when President William McKinley arrived to address the crowds. His visit drew nearly one hundred thousand people to the grounds in a single day, and the sitting orator and statesman William Jennings Bryan was also among the dignitaries who came at Wattles's invitation. For a city barely two generations removed from its frontier beginnings, hosting a president before so vast an audience was an unforgettable validation. When the gates closed on November 1, the white palaces were dismantled, and the lagoon and lawns gave way once more to ordinary city blocks. But the exposition left something that could not be torn down. It had proven that Omaha could organize, finance, and execute an event of national scale, and it filled the city with a lasting confidence that shaped its growth for decades to come. The fair's spirit of ambition and welcome can still be felt in the institutions and neighborhoods that visitors love today, from the historic Old Market to the city's grand civic landmarks. The New White City vanished in a single season, yet the pride it kindled became a permanent part of who Omaha is.
articles.excerpt: For one luminous summer and autumn, Omaha built a gleaming city of palaces around a shimmering lagoon and invited the world to come see the promise of the American West. The Trans-Mississippi and International Exposition of 1898 drew more than two and a half million visitors, a sitting president, and the largest gathering of Native nations of its era. It remains one of the proudest chapters in Omaha's story.
articles.seo_keywords: Trans-Mississippi Exposition, Omaha 1898 world's fair, New White City, Kountze Park, President McKinley Omaha, Indian Congress 1898, Omaha history, Grand Court lagoon

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: The Oracle of Omaha: How Warren Buffett Put Our City on the Map of American Finance
articles.body: Some cities chase greatness; Omaha simply raised it at home. When people around the world speak of the "Oracle of Omaha," they are paying tribute not only to one of history's most successful investors but to the city that shaped him and that he never left behind. Warren Buffett was born in Omaha on August 30, 1930, and the story of how a Nebraska boy grew into a steward of one of the world's most admired companies is, at heart, a story about Omaha itself, about the steady, unpretentious values that this prairie city quietly passes from one generation to the next. Buffett's roots run deep in Omaha soil. As a child during the Great Depression, he sold chewing gum and bottles of pop door to door, delivered newspapers across familiar neighborhoods, and developed the patient, practical sense for value that would define his life's work. The city was his first classroom, and its Midwestern temperament, hardworking, modest, and skeptical of flash, became the foundation of his philosophy. Even after studying on the East Coast and learning from celebrated mentors, he returned to Omaha to make his career, choosing the steady ground of home over the louder financial capitals that might have claimed him. That choice would change the city's story forever. The company that carries Omaha's reputation around the globe, Berkshire Hathaway, did not begin as a Nebraska enterprise at all. Its origins lie in the New England textile mills of the nineteenth century, a struggling cloth-making business that Buffett began buying into in 1962 and took control of in 1965. Over the decades that followed, he transformed that fading mill into a vast holding company, the textile operations eventually winding down in 1985 while the enterprise grew into one of the most valuable corporations in the world. Through every chapter of that remarkable growth, the headquarters remained in Omaha, in a famously plain office at Kiewit Plaza. There were no marble towers or coastal addresses, only a quiet suite of rooms in the heart of the city Buffett calls home. In keeping its base here, Berkshire Hathaway made Omaha a genuine capital of American finance, a place that proves great things need not be done in the biggest or loudest of cities. Perhaps nothing reflects Omaha's character more warmly than the way Buffett has chosen to live. Though his name appears among the wealthiest people on earth, he has stayed in the same gray stucco house in central Omaha that he bought in 1958 for a modest sum. He has long been known for ordinary pleasures and everyday routines, for treating neighbors as neighbors and for a modesty that feels distinctly homegrown. In a world that often equates success with extravagance, his example offers a gentler lesson, one that families here recognize instantly. It is the quiet confidence of a place that measures a person by character rather than display, and it has made Buffett not a distant celebrity but something closer to a beloved hometown figure. Visitors curious about the many remarkable lives this city has produced can explore more of Omaha's famous people and the threads that connect them. Once a year, the wider world comes to see Omaha for itself. Each spring, the Berkshire Hathaway annual shareholder meeting fills the city with tens of thousands of visitors from across the country and around the globe, an event so joyful and well attended that it has earned the affectionate nickname "Woodstock for Capitalists." What began as a tiny gathering of a handful of shareholders has grown into one of the largest meetings of its kind anywhere, drawing crowds that pack the downtown arena to listen, learn, and share in a sense of community. For a weekend, hotels brim, restaurants hum, and local shops welcome guests who often leave charmed by Omaha's friendliness and surprised by how much there is to enjoy. The gathering brings real warmth and energy to the city, a celebration that has become part of Omaha's modern heritage. The tale of Warren Buffett and Berkshire Hathaway is, in the end, a tale about staying true to a place. A city that began as a frontier crossroads, a story you can trace in how Omaha began, grew into a respected name in American business not by reinventing itself but by holding fast to its values. Omaha gave one of its sons his start, and he gave the city a place in financial history that no skyline could rival. For families who call this city home, that legacy is a quiet source of pride, a reminder that integrity, patience, and loyalty to one's roots can carry a person, and a city, remarkably far.
articles.excerpt: Long before Omaha was known as a capital of American finance, it was simply home to a curious boy named Warren Buffett. Discover how the Oracle of Omaha built one of the world's most admired companies without ever leaving the city he loves, and how he draws the world to our doorstep each spring.

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: Come to the Table: Why the Family Meal Still Shapes a Child's Heart
articles.body: Dear parent, if no one has told you lately that the ordinary table where your family eats is one of the most sacred places in your home, let this be the reminder. It does not look like much. It may be scratched and crowded, ringed with the faint marks of spilled milk and homework and a thousand hurried mornings. But around that table, more is happening than the filling of small stomachs. Hearts are being formed. And in a world that would gladly pull your family in a hundred different directions every evening, the simple decision to gather and eat together is quietly one of the most counter-cultural things you can do. There is a reason that so much of life, from the earliest pages of Scripture onward, happens around a shared meal. To sit down together is to say, without speaking it aloud, that we belong to one another. When you call your children to the table and they come, hands washed and chairs scraping the floor, you are teaching them a lesson far deeper than table manners. You are teaching them that they have a place that is theirs, a seat that stays empty when they are gone and fills with welcome when they return. Children who grow up with a reliable place at the table grow up with a reliable sense that they are wanted. That security does not come from a perfect meal or a perfectly behaved family. It comes from showing up, again and again. The table is also where conversation is learned, and conversation is the soil in which character grows. It is at dinner, more than anywhere else, that a child learns to wait his turn to speak, to ask a brother about his day, to listen when someone older tells a story he has heard before and laugh anyway. These are not small skills. They are the very habits of love, practiced in miniature, night after night, until they become second nature. A child who learns to pass the bread before serving himself is learning a posture of the heart that will serve him for the rest of his life. You cannot teach these things in a single lecture. You teach them slowly, by sitting close and letting the minutes add up. Perhaps the most important thing that happens at the family table is the thing easiest to overlook: nothing in particular. We live in an age that prizes the productive and the extraordinary, and there is a quiet pressure on parents to make every moment count, to turn even dinner into a lesson or an event. But children do not need their parents to be impressive. They need them to be near. The unremarkable evening meal, where the talk is mostly about who did what and what tomorrow holds, is the very ordinariness in which a child feels most at home. Years from now, your children will not remember the menu. They will remember the feeling of being gathered in, of belonging to something steady. If you would like to read more about this quiet work, you may find encouragement in our reflection on being present in a distracted world. This is also why the table is worth protecting from the glow of screens. When phones and tablets are set aside and faces can be seen, something is restored that no notification can replace. The table asks us to be where our bodies are, to give our full attention to the people in front of us, to let silence be comfortable and laughter be unhurried. Guarding that space is not about rules for their own sake. It is about refusing to let the most formative hour of the day be stolen by a thousand smaller, lesser things. A family that learns to set the screens aside at dinner is a family that has learned to choose one another on purpose. So set the table tonight, even if the meal is simple and the day was long. Light it with whatever warmth you have. Ask the small questions, say the short prayer of thanks, and let your children see that this gathering matters to you. You are not merely feeding a family. You are building one, one ordinary evening at a time, in a place that will outlast every passing thing the world tries to sell them. The table is waiting. Call them home to it, and trust that the quiet, faithful work you do there is shaping a love that will follow them long after they have grown and gone.
articles.excerpt: The ordinary table where your family eats is one of the most sacred places in your home. A warm letter to parents on how gathering for the evening meal forms a child's character, security, and lasting sense of belonging.
articles.seo_keywords: family dinner, family meal, raising children, character formation, screen-free family, sense of belonging, faith and family, parenting encouragement

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: Did You Know Omaha Is the Home of College Baseball?
articles.body: Here is a piece of trivia that every baseball fan should know: the single most important event in all of college baseball, the Men's College World Series, has called one city home for more than seventy years, and that city is Omaha, Nebraska. Every June, the eyes of the entire baseball world turn to Omaha, making it one of the most beloved sports traditions in America. The College World Series has been played in Omaha since 1950, an extraordinary run that has woven the event into the very identity of the city. For generations, the best college teams in the nation have traveled to Omaha each summer to compete for the championship, and the city has embraced the tournament like family. For decades the games were played at the legendary Rosenblatt Stadium, a name spoken with deep affection by longtime fans. Since 2011, the tournament has been held at the gleaming Charles Schwab Field in downtown Omaha, a beautiful modern ballpark built to host the event in style. What makes the Omaha connection so special is not just the longevity, but the way the whole city comes alive each June. The College World Series is not merely a tournament that happens to be in Omaha; it is a citywide celebration. Fans pour in from across the country, the downtown buzzes with energy, and the streets fill with families, food, music, and the unmistakable excitement of championship baseball. For many visitors, attending the College World Series in Omaha becomes a cherished annual tradition, a summer pilgrimage they would not miss. That is what makes this such a fun fact about Omaha. People who have never visited might not realize that this friendly Heartland city is, quite literally, the capital of college baseball, the place where champions are crowned year after year. It is a point of enormous local pride, and it draws tens of thousands of happy visitors to the city every single summer. And even outside of June, Omaha is a wonderful city for sports fans and families alike. The ballpark sits in a vibrant downtown full of restaurants, parks, and attractions, making it easy to turn a game into a whole weekend of fun. The festive, welcoming atmosphere that the College World Series is famous for is really just the Omaha spirit on full display, the same warmth and hospitality visitors find here all year round. So whether you are a die-hard baseball fan hoping to catch the College World Series in person, or simply a traveler looking for a fun, friendly, and surprising city to explore, Omaha delivers. Come experience the home of college baseball, soak up the summer excitement, and discover everything else this remarkable city has to offer. The next time someone asks where the College World Series is played, you will know the answer, and you will have a great reason to plan a trip. Before you come, see everything happening around the city on our Explore Omaha event calendar, and start planning your visit to the home of college baseball.
articles.excerpt: Every June, the eyes of the baseball world turn to one city for the College World Series, and it has called Omaha home since 1950. Here's the trivia behind the city's beloved summer tradition.

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: Did You Know Omaha Has the World's Largest Indoor Desert?
articles.body: Here is a trivia question that almost nobody guesses correctly: where would you find the largest indoor desert in the entire world? Not Arizona, not Nevada, not the Sahara. The answer is Omaha, Nebraska, where one of the most jaw-dropping attractions you can imagine sits under a giant glass dome at Omaha's Henry Doorly Zoo and Aquarium. It is called the Desert Dome, and it lives up to every part of that name. Step inside and you are transported into a sprawling indoor desert complete with rolling sand dunes, towering rock canyons, cacti, and the plants and animals of deserts from around the globe. It is so large and so realistic that it holds the world record as the largest indoor desert on the planet. And it is all housed beneath the largest glazed geodesic dome in the world, an architectural marvel that has become an instantly recognizable part of the Omaha skyline. The Desert Dome opened in 2002 and quickly became one of the zoo's signature attractions. But the wonders do not stop at the sand. Beneath the desert, in a hidden lower level called Kingdoms of the Night, visitors can explore the largest indoor nocturnal exhibit in the world, a mysterious realm of caves, swamps, and creatures of the dark. So in a single building, Omaha manages to hold not one but two remarkable world records, stacked literally on top of each other. And here is the thing: the Desert Dome is just one attraction inside what is widely considered one of the best zoos in the entire world. Omaha's Henry Doorly Zoo is famous far beyond Nebraska, regularly ranked among the top zoos anywhere, and it is packed with other record-setting wonders. There is the Lied Jungle, one of the largest indoor rainforests in the world, where you can walk through a steamy jungle full of monkeys and waterfalls. There is a vast aquarium, a butterfly pavilion, and so much more. Families can easily spend an entire day here and still not see it all. That is what makes this such a delightful piece of Omaha trivia. People often picture Nebraska as flat prairie and cornfields, never imagining that one of its cities is home to a world-class zoo with a record-breaking indoor desert, a record-breaking rainforest, and a record-breaking nocturnal kingdom all under one roof. It is exactly the kind of surprise that makes visiting Omaha so much fun. For families planning a trip, the Henry Doorly Zoo alone is worth the journey. It is consistently ranked as one of the top attractions in the Midwest, the kind of place that turns a weekend getaway into an unforgettable adventure for kids and grown-ups alike. Walking from a desert to a rainforest to the ocean depths, all in a single afternoon, is an experience you simply cannot get just anywhere. So the next time someone asks where to find the world's largest indoor desert, you will have a fun answer and a great reason to plan a visit. Come see the Desert Dome and the rest of Omaha's incredible zoo for yourself. Before you come, see everything else happening around the city on our Explore Omaha event calendar, and start planning a trip to the city with a desert under glass.
articles.excerpt: Under a giant glass dome in Omaha lies a record-setting desert, complete with sand dunes and towering cliffs. Here's the trivia behind one of the zoo's most jaw-dropping attractions.

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: Did You Know the TV Dinner Was Born in Omaha?
articles.body: Picture the classic TV dinner: a shiny aluminum tray divided into neat little compartments, turkey in one, peas in another, a scoop of mashed potatoes, and a sweet treat in the corner. It is one of the most iconic images of mid-century American life. But here is a piece of trivia that surprises almost everyone: the TV dinner as we know it was born right here in Omaha, Nebraska, and it all started with a giant turkey problem. The year was 1953, and Omaha was home to a thriving food company called C. A. Swanson and Sons, already well known across the country for its frozen poultry and chicken pot pies. That year, after disappointing Thanksgiving sales, Swanson found itself in a real pickle. The company was stuck with an astonishing surplus of leftover turkey, around 520,000 pounds of it. That is 260 tons of turkey, sitting in refrigerated railcars, with no obvious way to sell it. It was the kind of problem that could sink a business. But clever minds at the Omaha company turned that mountain of turkey into a stroke of genius. Inspired in part by the compartmentalized trays used to serve meals on airplanes, they came up with the idea of a complete, ready-to-heat frozen dinner packaged in a divided aluminum tray. They gave it a name that captured the spirit of the brand-new era of television sweeping American living rooms, and the Swanson TV Dinner was born. It hit grocery store freezers in September 1953, and it was an instant, runaway success. The idea was perfectly timed. Families were gathering around their new television sets, and a hot, complete dinner you could simply pop in the oven and eat on a tray in front of the TV fit the moment exactly. The TV dinner did not just sell out, it changed the way America ate, launching the entire frozen-meal industry that fills grocery store aisles to this day. And it all traces back to an Omaha company and a whole lot of leftover turkey. That is the kind of fun, only-in-Omaha story that makes this city such a delight to discover. Omaha has a long history as a powerhouse of American food and business, from the great stockyards that made it the meatpacking capital of the world to the famous steaks that still bear the city's name. The humble TV dinner is one more example of how this unassuming Heartland city has quietly shaped daily life for millions of Americans. For visitors, these tasty tidbits of history add a fun flavor to any trip. Omaha is a city that loves good food and good stories, and it has plenty of both. Come explore its restaurants, its history, and its surprising claims to fame, and you will never look at a frozen dinner the same way again. So the next time you slide a frozen meal into the oven, you can thank Omaha. Better yet, come taste the city's food scene for yourself. Before you visit, see everything happening around town on our Explore Omaha event calendar, and start planning a trip to the birthplace of the TV dinner.
articles.excerpt: That iconic foil tray with the turkey, peas, and potatoes? It got its start because of 260 tons of leftover Thanksgiving turkey and a clever Omaha company. Here's the tasty trivia.

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: Did You Know a U.S. President Was Born in Omaha?
articles.body: Quick, name the city where President Gerald Ford was born. Most people guess somewhere in Michigan, since that is where he grew up and built his political career. But the real answer is one of the most fun pieces of trivia in the whole country: the 38th President of the United States was born right here in Omaha, Nebraska. And the story only gets more surprising from there. Here is the part that stumps even history buffs. When he was born in Omaha on July 14, 1913, the future president was not named Gerald Ford at all. He came into the world as Leslie Lynch King Jr., born in a grand house on the corner where the Gerald R. Ford Birthsite and Gardens stands today. His name changed later in childhood after his mother, Dorothy, moved away and remarried a kind man named Gerald Ford, whose name the boy eventually took as his own. So the president America came to know as Gerald R. Ford began his life as a baby boy in Omaha with an entirely different name. That twist makes for one of the best trivia questions you can ask: which U.S. president was born under a different name in Omaha, Nebraska? Almost nobody gets it right, and almost everybody is delighted to learn the answer. Today, Omaha proudly remembers its presidential connection. The original house where he was born is no longer standing, but the site at 32nd and Woolworth has been turned into the lovely Gerald R. Ford Birthsite and Gardens, a peaceful spot with a memorial, a rose garden, and a model of the original home. For visitors, it is a charming and unexpected stop, a chance to stand on the very ground where an American president entered the world. It is the kind of place that makes you realize how much surprising history is tucked into this friendly Heartland city. This little-known fact is a perfect example of what makes Omaha such a fun place to explore. The city is full of these delightful surprises, stories that make you say, wait, that happened here? A president, a Hollywood legend, the most famous investor in the world, all with Omaha roots. For a city that often flies under the radar, Omaha has quietly played a starring role in American history again and again. If you love this kind of trivia, Omaha is the perfect place to dig up more of it. Come visit the Ford Birthsite and Gardens, wander the historic neighborhoods, and discover for yourself just how many famous firsts and surprising beginnings trace back to this city on the Missouri River. It is a wonderful destination for history lovers, curious families, and anyone who enjoys a good story, and there is always something happening to round out your trip. So the next time someone asks where Gerald Ford was born, you will know the fun answer, and you will have a great reason to plan a visit. Before you come, see everything happening around the city on our Explore Omaha event calendar, and start planning a trip to the birthplace of a president.
