articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: Did You Know the Reuben Sandwich Was Invented in Omaha?
articles.body: Few sandwiches are as beloved as the Reuben: warm, toasted rye bread piled high with corned beef, melted Swiss cheese, tangy sauerkraut, and a swipe of dressing, grilled to gooey perfection. It is a deli classic found on menus all across America. But here is a piece of trivia that may surprise you the next time you order one: many people believe the Reuben sandwich was invented right here in Omaha, Nebraska, and the story behind it is as fun as the sandwich is delicious. As Omaha lore tells it, the year was 1925, and the place was the elegant Blackstone Hotel. A group of local businessmen met there regularly for a late-night poker game, and one of the regulars was a grocer named Reuben Kulakofsky. During one of these games, as the story goes, the hungry players needed a hearty snack to keep them going, and Reuben dreamed up a stacked sandwich of corned beef, Swiss cheese, sauerkraut, and dressing on rye bread. It was a hit at the table. It was such a hit, in fact, that the hotel's owner, Charles Schimmel, who was part of the poker group, loved it so much that he added it to the menu of the hotel's restaurant. He named it the Reuben, after the man who created it, and the sandwich quickly developed a devoted following. From that single Omaha hotel, the Reuben spread to delis and diners across the nation, eventually becoming one of the most famous sandwiches in America. By the 1950s it had won a national sandwich contest, cementing its place as a true American classic. Now, it is worth a friendly note that a few other cities, including New York, have floated their own claims to the Reuben over the years, as often happens with beloved foods. But Omahans will tell you, with a grin and a great deal of pride, that their city is the true home of the Reuben, born at the Blackstone Hotel during a poker game nearly a century ago. It is a claim the city wears proudly, and the historic Blackstone neighborhood remains a fun, foodie-friendly part of town to this day. The Blackstone Hotel, by the way, has another delicious claim to fame. That same Omaha hotel is also credited with introducing Butter Brickle ice cream, the rich vanilla-and-toffee treat that became a nostalgic favorite. So this one elegant Omaha address may have given the world both a legendary sandwich and a classic ice cream flavor, which is not a bad legacy for a single hotel. This is exactly the kind of tasty trivia that makes Omaha such a fun city to visit. It is a place that has quietly contributed more to the American table than most people realize, from world-famous steaks to the TV dinner to the Reuben itself. Food lovers will find plenty to savor here, along with the stories that go with it. So the next time you bite into a Reuben, you can share the fun fact that it may well have been born in Omaha. Better yet, come taste the city's food scene yourself and visit the historic Blackstone district where it all began. Before you come, see everything happening around town on our Explore Omaha event calendar, and start planning a trip to the birthplace of the Reuben.
articles.excerpt: That stacked, toasty deli classic may have been dreamed up during a late-night poker game at an Omaha hotel in 1925. Here's the delicious trivia behind the Reuben sandwich.

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: Fontenelle Forest: A Wild and Wooded Escape Near Omaha
articles.body: Just minutes from the bustle of downtown Omaha, you can step out of the city and into a quiet, ancient world of towering trees, ferns, and birdsong. Fontenelle Forest, tucked along the Missouri River in neighboring Bellevue, is one of the great natural treasures of the Omaha area, and it has been protected and cherished for well over a century. For families, nature lovers, and anyone needing a breath of fresh air, it is one of the most rewarding places to spend a day. The story of Fontenelle Forest is a story of foresight and love for the land. Back in 1913, two visionary Omahans, Dr. A. A. Tyler and Dr. Harold Gifford Sr., founded the Fontenelle Forest Association with the goal of preserving the beautiful woodlands south of the city before they could be lost to development. It was a remarkable act of conservation for its time, and thanks to their efforts, this magnificent forest, more than a thousand acres of it, has remained wild and protected ever since. Generations later, we get to enjoy what they had the wisdom to save. Today, Fontenelle Forest offers visitors a chance to wander through one of the most scenic natural areas in the region. Miles of trails wind through the woods, including a wonderful system of raised wooden boardwalks that let you stroll easily through the forest and wetlands while keeping your feet dry and the delicate habitat undisturbed. As you walk beneath the canopy, you might spot deer, turtles, frogs, and an incredible variety of birds, for the forest is a renowned destination for birdwatchers, especially during the spring and fall migrations. Families especially love Fontenelle Forest. The nature center offers hands-on exhibits and friendly staff who help children connect with the natural world, and the gentle, well-maintained trails make it easy for visitors of all ages and abilities to explore. It is the kind of place where kids can discover the wonder of a forest firsthand, watching a heron stalk through a marsh or listening to the wind move through the treetops. In every season, the forest offers something new, from spring wildflowers and summer greenery to brilliant fall foliage and the quiet beauty of a snowy winter walk. What makes Fontenelle Forest such a delightful surprise for visitors is how close it is to the city. You can spend the morning exploring downtown Omaha's attractions and the afternoon deep in a peaceful forest, all without a long drive. It is a reminder that Omaha is a city beautifully balanced between vibrant urban life and easy access to nature, one of the many reasons it is such a pleasant place to visit. There is also something lovely about the deeper lesson of Fontenelle Forest. It exists today only because people more than a hundred years ago cared enough to protect it for a future they would never see. When you walk those boardwalks and breathe that clean, green air, you are enjoying a gift handed down across generations, the same kind of stewardship and care that runs through so much of Omaha's history. So when you plan your trip to Omaha, set aside time to explore Fontenelle Forest. Bring the family, bring your camera and a pair of binoculars, and discover the wild, wooded heart that sits right beside the city. Before you come, see everything else happening around town on our Explore Omaha event calendar, and start planning a visit that mixes the best of the city with the beauty of the great outdoors.
articles.excerpt: Just minutes from downtown Omaha lies an ancient forest of towering trees, winding boardwalks, and quiet wonder, protected for more than a century. Here is the story of Fontenelle Forest and why families love it.

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: The Stars Were Born Here: Famous People from Omaha
articles.body: For a city of its size, Omaha has produced a truly astonishing number of remarkable people. A legendary Hollywood dancer, a President of the United States, the most famous investor in the world, and a host of other notable names all got their start in this Heartland city. For visitors, discovering Omaha's famous sons and daughters is a delightful reminder that this unassuming city has had an outsized influence on American life. Here are just a few of the stars who were born here. Perhaps the most dazzling of all is Fred Astaire, born in Omaha in 1899. Astaire grew up to become one of the greatest dancers and entertainers in the history of film, a name synonymous with grace, elegance, and Hollywood's golden age. The boy from Omaha charmed the entire world with his effortless style, and more than a century after his birth, he remains a symbol of timeless class. It is a wonderful thing to think that one of the most graceful performers who ever lived first learned to walk on Omaha streets. Omaha can also claim a President of the United States. Gerald Ford, who served as the nation's 38th president, was born in Omaha in 1913. Though his family moved to Michigan when he was young, his life began right here, making Omaha the birthplace of an American president, a distinction few cities can claim. Today his birth site is remembered in the city, a point of quiet pride for Omahans. Then there is the man known the world over as the Oracle of Omaha. Warren Buffett, one of the most successful and respected investors in history, was born in Omaha in 1930, and unlike many who leave for the bright lights of the coasts, he famously stayed. Buffett still calls Omaha home, living modestly in the same city that shaped him and helping put it on the map as a center of business wisdom. His presence draws admirers from around the globe to Omaha each year, and his loyalty to his hometown speaks volumes about the city's appeal. The list goes on. Omaha was the birthplace of acclaimed actor Marlon Brando, born in 1930, who became one of the most influential performers of the twentieth century. The city has produced respected actors, musicians, athletes, writers, and business leaders across the generations. Even stars who were not born in Omaha found their beginnings here, the beloved actor Henry Fonda, a Nebraska native, famously got his early start on the stage at the Omaha Community Playhouse, which remains a thriving cultural institution to this day. What is it about Omaha that has produced so much talent? Perhaps it is the strong Heartland values, the supportive community, the good schools and theaters, and the grounded, hardworking spirit that runs through the city. Whatever the reason, the evidence is clear: Omaha has been quietly sending extraordinary people out into the world for well over a century, while welcoming visitors to come and see where it all began. For travelers, this rich heritage adds a fun layer to any visit. You can walk the same streets that a Hollywood legend once walked, visit the cultural institutions that launched great careers, and feel the creative, ambitious energy that still fills the city today. Omaha takes pride in its famous children, and exploring their stories is a charming way to get to know the place that shaped them. So when you plan your trip to Omaha, come discover the hometown of presidents, movie stars, and business legends for yourself. Explore its theaters, its neighborhoods, and its history, and see why this remarkable city keeps producing remarkable people. Before you come, see everything happening around town on our Explore Omaha event calendar, and start planning a visit to the city where the stars were born.
articles.excerpt: A Hollywood dancing legend, a President of the United States, and the world's most famous investor all share one hometown: Omaha. Here is a fun look at the remarkable people this city has given the world.

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: The Orpheum Theater: Omaha's Dazzling 1920s Palace
articles.body: Walk through the doors of the Orpheum Theater in downtown Omaha and you step into another world, a dazzling palace of gold leaf, marble, sparkling crystal chandeliers, and rich red velvet. For nearly a century, this breathtaking theater has been one of Omaha's crown jewels, and its story, from grand opening to near demolition to glorious rebirth, is one of the best in the city. The Orpheum opened its doors on October 10, 1927, in the golden age of the great American movie and vaudeville palaces. It was built at a cost of around two million dollars, an enormous sum for the time, and no expense was spared in making it spectacular. Nearly three thousand people attended the gala opening night, including the mayor and the famous Knights of Ak-Sar-Ben, all eager to see the lavish new showplace. In an era when theaters were designed to transport audiences into luxury the moment they walked inside, the Orpheum delivered, with soaring ceilings, ornate carvings, and an atmosphere of pure elegance. For decades the Orpheum hosted vaudeville acts, movies, and live performances, becoming a beloved gathering place for generations of Omahans. But like so many grand old theaters across America, it eventually fell on hard times. By the early 1970s, the building's future was in doubt, and a treasure that had taken sixteen months and a fortune to build was at risk of being lost forever. This is where Omaha's love for its own history saved the day. In 1973, the Knights of Ak-Sar-Ben, the same civic organization that had celebrated the theater's opening, stepped in and purchased the building. They invested about two million dollars to restore it to its former glory, and then made a generous gift of the theater to the city, on the condition that it be kept as a home for plays, musicals, and the performing arts. In 1975, the beautifully restored Orpheum reopened, rescued from the wrecking ball and given a brilliant new life. The city's commitment did not stop there. In 2001, a major ten-million-dollar renovation further restored and modernized the theater, ensuring that this historic palace could continue dazzling audiences for generations to come. Today, the Orpheum is operated as part of Omaha Performing Arts, and it remains one of the premier venues in the region, regularly hosting touring Broadway productions, concerts, ballet, opera, and more. What makes the Orpheum so special is that it is not a museum piece behind a velvet rope. It is a living, working theater where you can buy a ticket, settle into a plush seat beneath a glittering chandelier, and watch a world-class show in a space dripping with history and beauty. There is nothing quite like seeing a Broadway musical inside a genuine 1920s palace, surrounded by the same gilded splendor that wowed audiences nearly a hundred years ago. For a special night out, a date, or a family celebration, it is hard to beat. The Orpheum is also a wonderful symbol of what makes Omaha such a rewarding place to visit. This is a city that treasures its past and refuses to let its finest things slip away. Time and again, Omaha has chosen to save and restore its landmarks rather than tear them down, and visitors get to enjoy the rich, beautiful results. So when you plan a trip to Omaha, treat yourself to a show at the Orpheum Theater, or at the very least, admire its magnificent facade in the heart of downtown. Catch a Broadway tour, a concert, or a holiday performance, and experience the magic of a true theatrical palace. Before you come, see what is playing and everything else happening around the city on our Explore Omaha event calendar, and start planning an unforgettable night in Omaha.

articles.title: Standing Bear: The Omaha Trial That Changed America
articles.body: Some of the most important moments in American history happened in places you might not expect, and one of the most powerful of them all unfolded right here in Omaha. In the spring of 1879, in a federal courtroom in this city, a Native American chief named Standing Bear stood and spoke words that would change the course of American law forever. It is a story of courage and dignity, and it is one of the most meaningful reasons to know and visit Omaha. Standing Bear was a chief of the Ponca people, a tribe that had lived peacefully for generations along the Niobrara River in northern Nebraska. But in the 1870s, the United States government forced the Ponca from their homeland and removed them hundreds of miles south to Indian Territory, in what is now Oklahoma. The journey and the conditions there were devastating. Many of the Ponca died, including Standing Bear's own son. With his boy gone, Standing Bear made a heartfelt promise: he would carry his son's remains back to the homeland to be buried among his ancestors. And so, in the winter, Standing Bear and a small group of followers set out on the long walk north toward home. But when they reached the area near Omaha, the U.S. Army arrested them for leaving the territory without permission and held them at Fort Omaha, intending to send them back. It seemed their journey, and Standing Bear's promise, would end in failure. Then something remarkable happened. The story of these desperate travelers reached the people of Omaha, and the city responded with compassion. A newspaper editor publicized their plight, and two prominent local attorneys, John L. Webster and Andrew Poppleton, agreed to represent Standing Bear for free. They filed for a writ of habeas corpus, a basic legal protection against unlawful imprisonment, and the case came before Judge Elmer Dundy in the United States District Court in Omaha. The trial opened on April 30, 1879. The government made a chilling argument: it claimed that a Native American was neither a person nor a citizen under the law, and therefore had no right to sue or to seek the protection of the courts at all. The case would decide not just the fate of Standing Bear, but the very question of whether Native Americans were recognized as people under American law. At the close of the trial, Standing Bear himself was permitted to speak. In a moment that has echoed through history, he raised his hand and said that the blood that flowed in his hand was the same color as the blood in the judge's, that he was a man, and that the same God made them both. It was a simple, profound plea for his shared humanity to be recognized. Judge Dundy agreed. In his landmark ruling, he declared that an Indian is a person within the meaning of the law, and that Standing Bear was being held unlawfully and must be set free. It was the first time an American court had recognized that Native Americans were persons entitled to the protection of the law, a foundational moment in the long history of American civil rights. Standing Bear was freed, and he completed his journey, laying his son to rest in the homeland at last. This extraordinary story belongs to Omaha. It happened here, in this city, because ordinary people, an editor, two lawyers, a judge, and a community, chose compassion and justice. Today, Standing Bear is honored across the region, and his courage is remembered as one of the proudest chapters in the city's history. Visitors who come to Omaha can stand in the very place where this history unfolded and feel the weight and the inspiration of it. A trip to Omaha is a chance to connect with stories like this one, stories that shaped the nation and still have the power to move us. When you visit, take time to learn about Standing Bear and the history all around you. Before you come, see everything happening across the city on our Explore Omaha event calendar, and start planning a visit to the city where a brave man's words helped change America.
articles.excerpt: In an Omaha courtroom in 1879, a Ponca chief named Standing Bear won a case that changed American history forever. Here is the powerful, true story, and why it makes Omaha a city worth visiting.

articles.title: Joslyn Castle: Omaha's Storybook Mansion
articles.body: Tucked into a leafy historic neighborhood in midtown Omaha stands something you might not expect to find in the heart of the Great Plains: a genuine castle. With its grand stone towers, turrets, and carved details, Joslyn Castle looks like it stepped straight out of a storybook, and the tale of the remarkable couple who built it is every bit as memorable as the mansion itself. The castle was built in 1903 by George and Sarah Joslyn, one of the wealthiest and most generous couples in Omaha's history. George Joslyn had made a great fortune in the newspaper and printing business, and the couple chose to build a home worthy of a fairy tale. Designed in the elegant Scottish Baronial style and officially named Lynhurst, the mansion featured dozens of rooms filled with beautiful craftsmanship, set on grounds landscaped with gardens, greenhouses, and stately trees. It quickly became the centerpiece of the fashionable neighborhood that came to be known as the Gold Coast, and it remains one of the most distinctive historic homes in the entire region. But the Joslyns are remembered for far more than their grand house. They were extraordinarily devoted to Omaha and to giving back to their community. When George passed away, Sarah Joslyn carried their shared spirit of generosity forward in a way that would shape the cultural life of the city for generations. In her husband's memory, she funded the creation of the magnificent Joslyn Art Museum, a gift to the people of Omaha that remains one of the finest art museums in the country to this day. The same name that graces the castle also graces one of the city's greatest cultural treasures, a lasting testament to one family's love for their city. That connection is part of what makes Joslyn Castle so special to visit. It is not just a beautiful old mansion, but a window into the lives of people whose generosity still enriches Omaha more than a century later. Walking the grounds and admiring the stonework, you can imagine the gatherings once held here and feel the presence of a couple who believed that great wealth came with a responsibility to give back. Today, Joslyn Castle is lovingly preserved and listed on the National Register of Historic Places. It serves the community as a historic site and a venue for tours, events, and cultural gatherings, keeping its grand halls alive with activity rather than letting them fade into silence. For visitors, it offers the rare chance to step inside a true turn-of-the-century castle and experience the elegance of a bygone era, right in the middle of a modern American city. Joslyn Castle is a perfect example of the surprising delights that await travelers in Omaha. This is a city full of unexpected treasures, places that reward the curious visitor with beauty, history, and great stories. Pair a visit to the castle with a trip to the nearby Joslyn Art Museum, and you can experience both halves of the Joslyns' incredible legacy in a single, memorable day. So when you plan your trip to Omaha, make room for a visit to Joslyn Castle. Admire its storybook towers, learn the moving story of George and Sarah, and discover one of the city's most enchanting landmarks. Before you come, see everything happening across the city on our Explore Omaha event calendar, and start planning a visit to the city with a castle of its very own.
articles.excerpt: With its stone towers and turrets, Omaha really does have a castle, built in 1903 by one of the city's most generous couples. Here is the story of Joslyn Castle and the remarkable legacy of George and Sarah Joslyn.

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: Mutual of Omaha's Wild Kingdom: How an Omaha Company Brought the Wild Into America's Living Rooms
articles.body: For a whole generation of American families, Sunday evening had a familiar rhythm. Dinner finished, the dishes cleared, everyone gathered in the living room, and the television flickered to life with one of the most beloved programs of its era. It opened with wildlife and adventure, and with a name that became part of the national vocabulary: Mutual of Omaha's Wild Kingdom. What many viewers across the country never fully realized was that this window into the wonders of the natural world was brought to them by a company rooted right here in Omaha, Nebraska. Mutual of Omaha itself was already a proud part of the city long before it became a household name on television. Founded in Omaha back in 1909, the insurance company grew into one of the city's signature institutions, an anchor employer and a point of local pride for more than a century. But it was a bold leap into television that would make the Mutual of Omaha name famous in living rooms from coast to coast. In 1963, the company began sponsoring a wildlife program called Wild Kingdom, and it became a phenomenon. Hosted by the genial zoologist Marlin Perkins, alongside his energetic and fearless co-host Jim Fowler, the show took American families on adventures to see lions, elephants, crocodiles, and creatures they had only ever read about. Week after week, it carried viewers from their own couches to the savannas, jungles, and rivers of the world. For millions of children, it was their very first encounter with the great diversity of life on Earth, and for many of them, it sparked a lifelong love of animals and nature. Wild Kingdom was more than entertainment. In an age before endless nature documentaries and streaming wildlife footage, it played a real role in awakening Americans to the wonders of the natural world and the importance of caring for it. It made conservation feel exciting and personal. And it did all of this while carrying the name of an Omaha company proudly out into the world, so that for decades, people across America associated Omaha with adventure, wildlife, and Sunday nights with the family. The original series ran for years and became woven into the fabric of American pop culture, so much so that the phrase Mutual of Omaha's Wild Kingdom still brings a warm wave of nostalgia to anyone who grew up with it. The beloved brand has been revived for new audiences over the years, carrying its spirit of wonder to a new generation. And Mutual of Omaha remains a cornerstone of the city, its name and its iconic presence still very much part of Omaha today. There is something wonderful about the fact that one of the great shared memories of twentieth-century American family life came, in part, from Omaha. It is a reminder that this unassuming Heartland city has quietly shaped the wider culture in ways many people never realize, from the food on the nation's tables to the shows that gathered its families together. So when you visit Omaha, take a little pride in walking the streets of the city that sent the wild kingdom into America's living rooms. Explore the place that has shaped so much more of American life than its size would suggest. Before you come, see everything happening around the city on our Explore Omaha event calendar, and start planning your trip to this surprising and storied city.
articles.excerpt: For a generation of American families, Sunday evenings meant gathering around the TV for a wildlife show sponsored by an Omaha insurance company. Here is the story of Mutual of Omaha's Wild Kingdom, and the city pride behind it.

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: Lauritzen Gardens: Omaha's 100-Acre Botanical Paradise
articles.body: Tucked along the bluffs of the Missouri River sits one of Omaha's loveliest surprises, a sprawling botanical paradise that bursts with color from spring through fall and dazzles with lights in winter. Lauritzen Gardens, Omaha's botanical center, covers a hundred acres of gardens, woodlands, and walking paths, and it has quietly become one of the most beautiful places to spend a day in the entire region. What surprises most visitors is how young this gem actually is. Unlike many of the great botanical gardens of the world, which trace their roots back centuries, Lauritzen Gardens is a thoroughly modern achievement, a gift that the people of Omaha gave themselves. The gardens first opened in 1994 as the Omaha Botanical Gardens, beginning the patient, hopeful work of turning bluff-side land into something extraordinary. In 2001, a beautiful new visitor center opened and the gardens were renamed Lauritzen Gardens, in honor of the Lauritzen family, whose generosity helped make the vision real. From there, it only kept growing. And grow it has. In 2014, the gardens added the stunning Marjorie K. Daugherty Conservatory, a glass jewel box where lush tropical and desert plants thrive no matter the Nebraska weather outside, so you can wander through a rainforest in the middle of a snowy January. More recently, in 2025, the gardens opened Sofia's Play Garden, a delightful space designed to let children explore, climb, and fall in love with nature. With each addition, Lauritzen Gardens has become more of a destination and more of a treasure. A visit is a feast for the senses. You can stroll through formal gardens bursting with roses, wander woodland trails, admire the changing seasonal displays, and let the kids loose in spaces built just for them. Families especially love the charming model railroad garden, where miniature trains wind through tiny landscapes. And in the depths of winter, the gardens transform into a glittering wonderland of holiday lights that has become a cherished Omaha tradition, drawing crowds bundled up against the cold to walk among the glow. What makes Lauritzen Gardens so special is the way it blends beauty, learning, and peace. It is a place to slow down. On a warm afternoon, you can find people sketching among the flowers, families picnicking, couples strolling hand in hand, and children crouched low to watch a butterfly. In a busy world, it offers something increasingly rare: a hundred acres of calm, cultivated beauty, open to everyone. There is a lovely lesson in Lauritzen Gardens, too. It is proof that a great thing does not have to be old to be magnificent, and that a community willing to dream and to give can create lasting beauty in a single generation. A patch of riverside bluff became, in just a few decades, one of the finest gardens in the Midwest, all because the people of Omaha decided their city deserved it. So when you plan your trip to Omaha, set aside an afternoon for Lauritzen Gardens, whatever the season. Wander the paths, breathe in the flowers, bring the kids to play, and discover one of the most beautiful corners of the city. Before you come, see everything else happening around town on our Explore Omaha event calendar, and start planning a visit to Omaha's hundred-acre botanical paradise.
articles.excerpt: Tucked along the Missouri River bluffs is one of Omaha's most beautiful surprises: a 100-acre botanical paradise that is younger than you might think. Here is the story of Lauritzen Gardens, and why it belongs on every Omaha visitor's list.

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: Winter Quarters: The Pioneers' Hard Omaha Winter and Historic Florence
articles.body: Before Omaha was a city, before its founding picnic in 1854, the bluffs along the Missouri River witnessed one of the most moving and difficult chapters in the entire story of the American frontier. In the autumn of 1846, thousands of weary pioneers arrived on these banks, and many of them would never leave. Their story is preserved today in the historic Omaha neighborhood of Florence, and it is one of the most meaningful places a visitor can go in the whole city. The pioneers were members of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, often called the Mormons, and they were in the middle of an extraordinary and perilous journey. Driven from their homes in the east by persecution, tens of thousands of them had set out on a great westward migration toward a new home in the distant Rocky Mountains. But the journey was too long to complete in a single season, and as winter closed in during 1846, they needed somewhere to stop, shelter, and survive. They chose the western bank of the Missouri, on land that would one day become part of Omaha, and they called their settlement Winter Quarters. What they built there was remarkable. In a matter of weeks, the pioneers raised a temporary city of hundreds of log cabins and dugouts, complete with streets, a mill, and a sense of order, all to carry thousands of people through the coming cold. But the winter of 1846 and 1847 was merciless. Bitter cold, poor food, exhaustion, and disease swept through the crowded settlement, and despite their faith and their determination, the suffering was terrible. By the time spring arrived, around six hundred of them had died and been buried on the bluff above the river, far from the homes they had left and the home they were still seeking. In the spring of 1847, the survivors pressed on. Brigham Young led the first company westward toward the valley that would become their new home, and over the following years, the trail that ran through Winter Quarters carried wave after wave of pioneers on the same long road. The little winter city had served its purpose, a way station of both shelter and sorrow on one of the great migrations in American history. Today, that ground is sacred and beautifully preserved. The Mormon Pioneer Cemetery crowns the bluff, marked by a moving monument that honors those who died during that brutal winter, a quiet and powerful place for reflection. Nearby, the Mormon Trail Center at Historic Winter Quarters welcomes visitors of all backgrounds to learn the full story through exhibits and warm hospitality. Standing there, looking out over the river the pioneers once gazed upon, it is impossible not to feel the weight of their courage and their loss. The surrounding neighborhood of Florence is itself one of the oldest communities in the Omaha area, with its own rich history and historic landmarks that predate the city's founding. Together, Florence and Winter Quarters offer a window into the deep past of this place, a reminder that the land Omaha stands on was a crossroads of American history long before the city had a name. A visit here is different from a trip to a museum or a park. It is a chance to stand on hallowed ground and to honor the faith, endurance, and sacrifice of ordinary people who faced an extraordinary trial. It is history that stays with you. So when you come to Omaha, set aside time for Winter Quarters and historic Florence. Before you visit, see everything else happening around the city on our Explore Omaha event calendar, and start planning a trip that includes one of the most moving places in Omaha.
articles.excerpt: Before Omaha was even a city, thousands of pioneers spent a brutal winter on these riverbanks, and hundreds never left. Here is the moving story of Winter Quarters and historic Florence, one of the most meaningful places to visit in Omaha.
articles.seo_keywords: Winter Quarters Omaha, Florence Omaha, Mormon Trail Center, visit Omaha, things to do in Omaha, Omaha history, Mormon pioneers Nebraska, Omaha tourist attractions, history of Omaha Nebraska, great places in Omaha

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: Ak-Sar-Ben: The Quirky Story Behind One of Omaha's Most Beloved Traditions
articles.body: Here is a fun little puzzle that unlocks one of Omaha's most charming secrets. Take the strange-looking word Ak-Sar-Ben, the name attached to a thriving Omaha district, a historic arena, and a century-old civic tradition, and read it backwards. Ak-Sar-Ben spells Nebraska. That playful bit of wordplay, dreamed up well over a century ago, is the key to one of the most beloved and uniquely Omaha stories there is. It began in 1895. Omaha's business leaders, organized as the Commercial Club, the forerunner of today's Chamber of Commerce, wanted to lift the city's spirits and boost its economy. They created a festive civic organization to celebrate Omaha and draw people downtown, and with a wink of Heartland humor, they named it after the state spelled backwards: the Knights of Ak-Sar-Ben. What started as a clever promotional idea grew into one of the most enduring traditions in the city's history. The Knights threw themselves into making Omaha shine. They staged a grand annual fall festival with parades and pageantry that drew crowds from across the region. At the heart of it all was a spectacular coronation ball, a glittering event at which a King and Queen, drawn from the community, were crowned amid royal ceremony, surrounded by elaborately costumed attendants and an air of fairy-tale grandeur. For generations of Omaha families, being part of the Ak-Sar-Ben court was a treasured honor, and the coronation remains a cherished tradition to this day. Ak-Sar-Ben also gave Omaha some of its most beloved gathering places. The organization built the Ak-Sar-Ben racetrack in 1920 and the grand Ak-Sar-Ben Coliseum in 1929, and for decades these venues were the beating heart of the city's entertainment. The Coliseum hosted concerts, sporting events, rodeos, ice shows, and the famous stock show, while the racetrack drew enormous crowds. If you grew up in Omaha in the twentieth century, chances are some of your fondest memories happened at Ak-Sar-Ben. Like all things, that era eventually changed. The racetrack closed its gates in the 1990s, and for a time it seemed an Omaha institution might simply fade into memory. But the city did what Omaha so often does with its history: rather than let it disappear, it reinvented it. The old Ak-Sar-Ben grounds were transformed into Aksarben Village, a vibrant, modern mixed-use district full of shops, restaurants, offices, apartments, and a university campus, all gathered around the lovely Stinson Park, where summer concerts and farmers markets now draw happy crowds. The name lived on, the spirit lived on, and a beloved old place was reborn for a new generation. Through all of it, the Knights of Ak-Sar-Ben never stopped doing good. The organization has long devoted itself to philanthropy and to building up the community, quietly supporting countless causes across Nebraska and pouring its energy back into the city it was created to celebrate. More than a century after a group of boosters spelled Nebraska backwards for fun, their creation is still making Omaha a better place. That is the delightful heart of the Ak-Sar-Ben story: a city that can laugh at itself, dream big, honor its past, and turn even a silly backwards-spelled name into a century of pride, celebration, and generosity. When you visit Omaha, stroll through Aksarben Village, relax in Stinson Park, and enjoy a district that carries a wonderfully Omaha story in its very name. Before you come, see everything happening around the city on our Explore Omaha event calendar, and start planning your visit to a city with a great sense of humor and an even greater heart.
articles.excerpt: Read its name backwards and you will unlock one of Omaha's best-kept secrets. Here is the delightful story of Ak-Sar-Ben, the civic tradition with the funny name that has shaped Omaha for more than a century.
articles.seo_keywords: Ak-Sar-Ben Omaha, Aksarben Village, Knights of Ak-Sar-Ben, visit Omaha, things to do in Omaha, Omaha tourist attractions, history of Omaha Nebraska, Omaha traditions, Stinson Park Omaha, great places in Omaha

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: How Omaha Became the Cattle Capital of the World
articles.body: If you have ever enjoyed a famous Omaha steak, you have tasted the legacy of one of the most important chapters in the city's history. For much of the twentieth century, Omaha was not just a meatpacking town; it was, for a time, the meatpacking capital of the entire world, surpassing even the mighty Chicago. The story of how a young river city earned that crown is the story of railroads, immigrants, and an enormous district of pens and packinghouses on the city's south side. It began in 1883, when the Union Stockyards opened in South Omaha. The timing was no accident. Omaha had already become a great railroad hub, the eastern anchor of the transcontinental line, and the railroads made it possible to bring cattle, hogs, and sheep in from across the plains and ship meat back out to feed a growing nation. The stockyards gave all that livestock a place to be bought and sold, and almost overnight, an industry exploded around them. The great meatpacking companies of the age, names like Armour, Swift, and Cudahy, built sprawling plants right beside the yards, and South Omaha boomed into a city within a city. The work was hard and often grueling, and it drew waves of immigrants hungry for a new start. Poles, Czechs, Irish, Italians, Lithuanians, and many others poured into South Omaha to work the yards and the packinghouses, and they brought their churches, their languages, their food, and their traditions with them. They built tight-knit neighborhoods that gave the area a rich, diverse character it still carries proudly today. The industry grew so large that in 1955, Omaha achieved something remarkable: it overtook Chicago to become the largest livestock market and meatpacking center in the world. For a city that had begun as a frontier picnic just a century earlier, it was an astonishing rise. Omaha held that world title until 1971, and the stockyards themselves kept running until 1999, when, after one hundred and sixteen years, the great yards finally closed. The pens and the packinghouses may be mostly gone, but their legacy is everywhere in Omaha. It lives in the city's enduring reputation for outstanding steaks and its beloved steakhouses, a point of genuine local pride. It lives in the historic Livestock Exchange Building, the grand old headquarters of the stockyards that still stands as a landmark. And most of all, it lives in the vibrant neighborhoods of South Omaha, where the descendants of those hardworking immigrant families have kept their cultures alive through festivals, restaurants, churches, and markets that are a joy to explore. That is the wonderful thing about this history: you can still taste it and walk through it. A visit to South Omaha offers some of the most authentic and flavorful experiences in the city, a living reminder of the immigrant grit and ambition that built Omaha into a world capital. And of course, no trip to the city that fed a nation would be complete without sitting down to a proper Omaha steak. So when you visit Omaha, take some time to explore this hardworking heritage. Wander South Omaha, sample its incredible food, admire the old Livestock Exchange Building, and raise a fork to the immigrants who made Omaha the cattle capital of the world. Before you come, see everything happening around the city on our Explore Omaha event calendar, and start planning a visit to the city that once fed America.
articles.excerpt: For much of the twentieth century, Omaha was the meatpacking capital of the world, bigger even than Chicago. Here is the story of the Omaha Stockyards, the immigrants who built South Omaha, and the legacy you can still taste today.

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: Omaha's RiverFront: How a Beloved Downtown Park Was Reborn
articles.body: If you have not been to downtown Omaha in a few years, you have not really seen it, because the heart of the city has just been reborn. Along the Missouri River, where Omaha began more than a century and a half ago, the city has completed one of the most ambitious park transformations in its history, and the result is a dazzling, family-friendly destination that did not exist in this form even a few summers ago. To appreciate how special it is, you have to know the long road that led here. The story really begins back in the 1970s. Downtown Omaha was struggling as businesses drifted away, and a mayor named Gene Leahy had a bold idea: bring the city back to the river. He championed a plan to turn neglected downtown blocks into a green oasis, and the result, eventually named the Gene Leahy Mall in his honor, became a beloved sunken park with a winding lagoon and a set of metal slides that generations of Omaha kids grew up flying down. It helped spark decades of downtown revival. By the 2010s, though, the well-loved park was showing its age, and the city set its sights on something far grander. Rather than simply patch up one park, Omaha launched a sweeping plan to reimagine three connected downtown green spaces, the Gene Leahy Mall, Heartland of America Park, and Lewis and Clark Landing, as one unified seventy-two-acre destination known as The RiverFront. The price tag was enormous, around three hundred and twenty-five million dollars, a sign of just how seriously the city believed in its downtown. The Gene Leahy Mall closed in 2019 for its dramatic makeover, and Omahans waited as the old sunken park was completely transformed. In July of 2022, it reopened, and the change was breathtaking. The park had been raised up to street level, opening it to the city around it, with sprawling new lawns, a performance pavilion for concerts, a state-of-the-art playground, interactive water features, a sculpture garden, and even a sizeable dog park. And in a wonderful nod to the past, the beloved slides were preserved, so a new generation can fly down the very same hills their parents did. What makes the new RiverFront so special is how completely it has redefined what a visit to downtown Omaha can be. On a warm day, the lawns fill with families, kids splash in the water features, music drifts from the pavilion, and the city skyline rises beautifully beyond the green. It is the kind of place where you could happily spend an entire afternoon, then walk just a few minutes to the Old Market for dinner. The city did not just renovate a park; it gave its downtown a brand-new front porch. There is something poetic about it, too. More than a century and a half after Omaha was born on the banks of the Missouri, the city has returned, grandly and joyfully, to the very river where it all began, fulfilling a dream first dreamed half a century ago. The past and the future meet right there on the riverfront. So if you are looking for a reason to visit Omaha right now, this is it. Come see the brand-new RiverFront, let the kids loose on the playground and the famous slides, and experience a downtown that has never looked better. Before you plan your trip, take a look at everything happening around the city on our Explore Omaha event calendar, and start planning a visit to Omaha's beautiful reborn riverfront.
articles.excerpt: A half-century after one mayor dreamed of bringing downtown Omaha back to the river, the city completed a $325 million transformation of its riverfront parks. Here is the story of Gene Leahy Mall and The RiverFront, and why it is the freshest reason to visit Omaha.
articles.seo_keywords: Omaha RiverFront, Gene Leahy Mall, things to do in Omaha, visit Omaha, downtown Omaha parks, Omaha tourist attractions, new in Omaha, Omaha family travel, history of Omaha Nebraska, great places in Omaha

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: The Bob: Omaha's Bridge Where You Can Stand in Two States at Once
articles.body: Here is a simple, delightful thing you can do in Omaha that you cannot do in very many places on earth: stand with one foot in Nebraska and the other in Iowa at the exact same time. The spot is the middle of the Bob Kerrey Pedestrian Bridge, a graceful white ribbon of a bridge that arcs across the Missouri River, and it has quickly become one of the most beloved and photographed places in the whole city. The bridge, affectionately nicknamed the Bob by locals, opened in 2008 as a bold piece of the city's long effort to reconnect with its riverfront. Stretching across the wide Missouri, it links Omaha, Nebraska on one bank with Council Bluffs, Iowa on the other, joining the two riverside communities with a beautiful pathway just for people on foot and on bikes. It is one of the longest pedestrian bridges in the country to connect two states, and crossing it is a small adventure in itself. The design is genuinely striking. Tall pylons rise from the bridge and fan out a web of cables that hold the curving deck aloft, giving the whole structure the look of a sail catching the wind. As you walk out over the water, the city skyline spreads out behind you, the river slides by beneath, and the green banks stretch away on either side. At sunrise or sunset, with the light glinting off the water and the cables, it is one of the most beautiful views in Omaha, and a favorite of photographers. But the bridge's most famous feature is also its most playful. Right at the midpoint, a marker shows the state line, the invisible boundary between Nebraska and Iowa, running straight across the deck. Families line up to take the classic photo, one foot planted firmly in each state, grinning at the simple joy of being in two places at once. Kids love it, and so do the grown-ups pretending they are too cool to love it. The Bob is more than a novelty, though. It anchors a growing network of riverside trails on both sides of the river, making it a favorite of walkers, runners, and cyclists. It connects directly to the parks and paths of Omaha's revitalized riverfront, so a stroll across the bridge can easily become an afternoon of exploring. And because it is free and open to everyone, it has become a true gathering place, a spot for first dates, family outings, morning jogs, and quiet evening walks with the city glowing in the distance. There is something wonderfully fitting about the Bob. Omaha was born on this river, grew up alongside it, and for years had turned its back on it. A graceful pedestrian bridge that invites people to walk right out over the water, to slow down and take in the view and stand happily between two states, is a perfect symbol of a city that has rediscovered the river at its heart. So when you visit Omaha, do not miss the chance to walk the Bob. Bring the family, snap the two-states photo, soak in the skyline, and feel the breeze off the Missouri. Before you plan your trip, see everything happening around the city on our Explore Omaha event calendar, and start planning a visit to one of Omaha's most fun and photogenic landmarks.
articles.excerpt: On Omaha's riverfront, a graceful curving bridge lets you put one foot in Nebraska and the other in Iowa at the very same time. Here is the story of the Bob Kerrey Pedestrian Bridge, and why it is one of the most fun, family-friendly stops in the city.

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: The Strategic Air Command & Aerospace Museum: Cold War Giants Near Omaha
articles.body: Most visitors are surprised to learn that for much of the twentieth century, the Omaha area was one of the most important places on the planet. Just south of the city sits Offutt Air Force Base, and for decades Offutt was the headquarters of the Strategic Air Command, the nerve center of America's Cold War air power. The decisions made here helped hold a tense and dangerous peace, and the aircraft that flew under this command became legends. Today, you can stand right beneath those legends at the Strategic Air Command and Aerospace Museum. To understand the museum, you have to picture the era it preserves. During the Cold War, the Strategic Air Command was responsible for the bombers and missiles that formed the backbone of America's deterrent, and Omaha was its brain. It was serious, high-stakes work, carried out quietly by thousands of airmen, and it placed this corner of Nebraska at the very center of world events. The museum exists to remember that history and to honor the people who lived it. The collection first took shape near the base itself, but in 1998 it moved into a striking modern facility along the interstate near Ashland, an easy and scenic drive between Omaha and Lincoln. The new home gave these enormous machines the space they deserved, and walking into the main hangars for the first time is genuinely jaw-dropping. The aircraft are not roped off behind glass. They tower right over you. And what aircraft they are. The museum is home to dozens of historic planes and missiles, including the almost unbelievable SR-71 Blackbird, the fastest jet aircraft ever built, a sleek black spy plane that looks like it flew straight out of the future. There are mighty bombers, nimble fighters, towering missiles, and aircraft that played roles in some of the most important moments of the last century. Standing beneath them, you feel the sheer scale and ambition of the age that produced them. What makes the museum especially wonderful is that it is built for families, not just for aviation buffs. Kids are captivated by the size and power of the machines, and the museum offers hands-on exhibits, a dedicated children's area, and programs designed to spark young imaginations. It is the kind of place where a child might decide, right there on the hangar floor, that they want to be a pilot or an engineer someday. Parents, meanwhile, find themselves just as amazed as the kids. There is real meaning here, too, beyond the spectacle. The museum tells a story of service and sacrifice, of ingenuity and courage, and of a community that quietly carried an enormous responsibility for the nation. It is history you can reach out and almost touch, and it leaves visitors with a deeper appreciation for the people who served. So if you are bringing the family to Omaha, set aside a few hours for the Strategic Air Command and Aerospace Museum, just a short drive from the city. Walk beneath the giants, let the kids dream big, and take in a remarkable chapter of American history. Before you plan your visit, see everything else happening around the area on our Explore Omaha event calendar, and start building a trip the whole family will remember.
articles.excerpt: For decades, the Omaha area was the command center of America's Cold War air power. Today you can walk beneath the legendary aircraft that defined that era. Here is the story of the Strategic Air Command & Aerospace Museum, and why families love it.
articles.seo_keywords: SAC Museum Omaha, Strategic Air Command Aerospace Museum, things to do in Omaha with kids, visit Omaha, Omaha tourist attractions, Offutt Air Force Base history, Omaha family travel, SR-71 Blackbird, history of Omaha Nebraska, great places near Omaha

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: Boys Town: How One Priest's Belief in Every Child Built an Omaha Legend
articles.body: Just west of downtown Omaha sits a place that began with almost nothing and grew into one of the most inspiring stories in America. It started in 1917, when a young Irish-born Catholic priest named Father Edward J. Flanagan looked at the homeless and forgotten boys of the city, the runaways and the orphans and the ones everyone else had given up on, and refused to believe they were beyond hope. With a borrowed house and a few dollars he could scarcely spare, he opened a home for them, and he built it on a single radical idea that would echo for a century: that there is no such thing as a bad boy, only a child who has not yet been loved and guided well. Father Flanagan's home grew quickly, far faster than his tiny budget could comfortably hold. In 1921, he moved his expanding family of boys to Overlook Farm on the western edge of Omaha, and there something remarkable took shape. It was not merely an orphanage. Flanagan built a real community, a self-governing village where the boys had their own elected leaders, their own schools, their own chapel, and a sense of belonging that most had never known. The boys learned trades, played sports, worshiped, and discovered that someone believed in them. The place became known simply as Boys Town. For years it was Omaha's quiet miracle, but in 1938 the whole world learned about it. Hollywood released a film called Boys Town, with Spencer Tracy playing Father Flanagan in a performance so moving it won him an Academy Award. Suddenly Flanagan's little Nebraska village was famous everywhere, and donations and admiration poured in from across the globe. Out of that fame came one of the most beloved images in American culture: the statue of an older boy carrying a younger one on his back, beneath the words, He ain't heavy, Father, he's my brother. It captured everything Flanagan believed in a single picture. What is most remarkable is that Boys Town never became a museum piece. It is still very much alive and still doing the work Father Flanagan began. Today it cares for children across the country, and it is home to the renowned Boys Town National Research Hospital, a leader in helping kids with serious medical needs. A simple idea born in an Omaha rented house in 1917 grew into a national force for good that has touched the lives of countless children. And here is the wonderful part for anyone planning a trip to Omaha: you can visit. The historic village of Boys Town is open to the public, and walking its peaceful campus is a genuinely moving experience. You can step inside the beautiful Dowd Memorial Chapel, explore the Hall of History that tells Father Flanagan's story, and see for yourself the famous statue that has comforted so many. Families especially love a quirky treasure on the campus, the World's Largest Ball of Stamps, a delightful surprise that kids never forget. There is something about Boys Town that stays with you. In an age that can feel cynical, it stands as living proof of what one person's faith and stubborn love can build, and of the truth that every child is worth fighting for. It is history you can walk through, hope you can see with your own eyes. So when you plan your visit to Omaha, make room for Boys Town. Bring the family, take your time, and let the story sink in. Before you come, see everything else happening around the city on our Explore Omaha event calendar, and start planning a trip that includes one of the most inspiring landmarks in America.
articles.excerpt: It began in 1917 with a young priest, a rented house, and a radical belief: that there is no such thing as a bad boy. That belief grew into Boys Town, one of Omaha's most inspiring landmarks. Here is the story, and why you should visit.
articles.seo_keywords: Boys Town Omaha, Father Flanagan, things to do in Omaha, visit Omaha, Boys Town history, Omaha tourist attractions, history of Omaha Nebraska, Omaha family travel, great places in Omaha, no such thing as a bad boy

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: The Durham Museum: Step Inside Omaha's Magnificent Union Station
articles.body: Walk into the Durham Museum in Omaha and look up, and you may forget to keep walking. The Great Hall soars overhead in gleaming Art Deco splendor, with a richly decorated ceiling, tall arched windows that pour in golden light, glittering chandeliers, and a polished floor that stretches out like a ballroom. It is, without exaggeration, one of the most beautiful rooms in the entire region. And to truly appreciate it, you have to know that this magnificent space was built not as a palace, but as a train station, and that it very nearly disappeared. The building opened in 1931 as Omaha's Union Station, constructed by the Union Pacific Railroad in the height of the Art Deco era. In those days, the railroad was the lifeblood of travel, and a city's train station was its grand front door. Union Station was Omaha's, a soaring temple of travel where countless people began journeys, came home from war, said goodbye to loved ones, and stepped off into a new life. For decades, the Great Hall echoed with the footsteps and farewells of a nation on the move. But as the twentieth century wore on, America fell in love with the automobile and the airplane, and the age of passenger rail faded. By 1971, the trains that had filled Union Station slowed to a stop, and the great building fell silent. It could easily have met the fate of so many beautiful old stations across the country, neglected and eventually torn down. Omaha, thankfully, had other plans. Union Pacific gave the building to the city, and rather than lose it, Omaha reinvented it. In 1975 the old station reopened with a brand-new purpose, as a museum dedicated to the region's history. In time, thanks to the generosity of the Durham family, it became the Durham Museum, the name it carries proudly today. The very space built to send people out into the world was now devoted to preserving the world they came from. Today the Durham Museum is one of Omaha's crown jewels and a Smithsonian Affiliate, which means it regularly hosts world-class traveling exhibitions you might otherwise have to visit a coast to see. But its heart is the lovingly restored Great Hall itself, complete with bronze statues of 1930s travelers frozen mid-journey, an old-fashioned soda fountain, and exhibits that carry you through the rich story of Omaha and the people who built it. Children love exploring the vintage train cars, and visitors of every age find themselves slowing down, looking up, and feeling the weight of all the lives that passed through. That is the magic of the Durham. It is a place where history is not behind glass so much as all around you, in the very walls and ceiling and light. You are not just learning about the past; you are standing inside a piece of it that a wise city refused to throw away. So when you plan your trip to Omaha, put the Durham Museum near the top of your list, and be sure to look up the moment you step into that hall. Bring the kids, take your time, and let one of America's most beautiful old stations work its quiet magic on you. Before you come, check everything happening around the city on our Explore Omaha event calendar, and start planning a visit built around this Omaha treasure.
articles.excerpt: One of the most beautiful rooms in Omaha is the soaring Art Deco hall of an old train station that almost disappeared. Here is the story of Union Station and the Durham Museum, and why it belongs at the top of your Omaha itinerary.

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: Raising Kind Children: Teaching Compassion in a World That Rewards the Loud
articles.body:
Dear parent,

If there is one thing I would ask you to plant in your child's heart above almost any other, it would be kindness. Not the soft, sentimental kind we put on greeting cards, but the sturdy, deliberate kindness that notices the lonely child at the edge of the playground and walks over anyway. In a world that so often rewards the loud, the clever, and the quick, raising a tender heart can feel like swimming against the current. Yet I am convinced it is one of the most important and lasting works a parent ever does, and I want to encourage you that it is within your reach, even on the ordinary days when you feel you have little left to give.

Kindness is not a personality trait that some children are simply born with and others lack. It is a habit of the heart, and habits are taught. A child learns compassion the same way he learns to tie his shoes or speak his first words, through patient repetition and the steady example of someone who loves him. This is good news, because it means kindness does not depend on whether your child is naturally outgoing or shy, spirited or gentle. Every child can be taught to consider others, and every parent has the standing to teach it. You do not need a degree or a perfect home. You need only the willingness to model what you hope to see.

And modeling is where it begins. Our children are forever watching us when we think they are not. They see how we speak to the tired cashier, how we treat the family member who is difficult to love, whether we grumble about the neighbor or carry a meal to her door. They learn far more from the kindness we live than the kindness we lecture about. If we want children who are gentle with the weak and patient with the slow, we must let them catch us being gentle and patient ourselves. The words "be kind" carry little weight when a child has just watched us snap at the person who cut us off in traffic. But when our daily conduct quietly matches our instruction, the lesson sinks down into the bones.

It also helps to give kindness somewhere to go. Compassion grows muscle through use, and children need real chances to practice it. Let your child carry the groceries for an elderly neighbor, write a note to a grandparent, share a toy that costs him something to share. When you serve others as a family, you are not merely doing a good deed; you are showing your child that his hands were made to lift others up. The Scriptures remind us that we are to love our neighbor as ourselves, and a child begins to understand that ancient command not by hearing it explained but by living it out in small, concrete acts that become second nature over time.

I would also gently encourage you to teach your child that kindness includes those who can offer nothing in return. It is easy to be generous with friends and those we hope to impress. The deeper work is to notice the overlooked, to befriend the child no one else will sit with, to speak gently to the person the world has decided does not matter. When you point your child toward the forgotten and the struggling, you are teaching him that every person carries dignity and worth. This is the kindness that changes the world, and it begins at your kitchen table long before it ever reaches the schoolyard.

Be patient, too, with the slow progress. Children are still children, and they will be selfish one hour and saintly the next. A kind child is not a finished child but a growing one, and your job is not to demand perfection but to keep gently turning his face toward others. When he fails, and he will, do not shame him. Show him the better way, forgive him as you hope to be forgiven, and try again tomorrow. The goal is not a child who performs kindness for praise but one whose heart has been slowly shaped to care.

So take heart. The quiet investments you are making, the apologies you model, the compassion you show when no one is keeping score, are not wasted. You are raising a person who will one day carry comfort into rooms you will never enter, who will be gentle with people you will never meet. Few labors matter more, and few are more worthy of your weary, faithful effort. Keep going. The tender heart you are cultivating today will bless this world long after the lessons are forgotten and only the love remains.
articles.excerpt: Kindness isn't a trait some children are born with and others lack — it's a habit of the heart, and habits are taught. A warm letter to parents on raising compassionate children by modeling gentleness, giving kindness room to grow, and teaching them to love even the overlooked.
articles.seo_keywords: raising kind children, teaching compassion to kids, kindness in parenting, raising compassionate children, teaching children empathy, character building for kids, faith-based parenting, gentle parenting

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: Sarah Joslyn and the Pink Marble Gift That Became Omaha's Art Museum
articles.body: Of all the beautiful buildings in Omaha, few are as breathtaking as the Joslyn Art Museum, a gleaming temple of pale pink marble that rises with quiet elegance near the heart of the city. It is the largest art museum in Nebraska and one of the finest in the region. But the most beautiful thing about the Joslyn is not its marble or its galleries. It is the love story behind it. The story begins with George and Sarah Joslyn. George Joslyn was a self-made businessman who built a fortune in the newspaper industry and became, in his day, the richest man in Nebraska. He and his wife Sarah shared a deep love for the arts and for the city that had given them so much. When George died in 1916, Sarah was left not only with grief, but with a fortune and a vision for how to honor the man she had loved. Rather than build a monument to herself or simply pass her wealth quietly along, Sarah Joslyn decided to give Omaha something that would lift up its people for generations. In 1928 she donated more than three million dollars, an astonishing sum at the time, to build a grand arts center in George's memory. It would be a place where the citizens of Omaha, rich and poor alike, could come and be surrounded by beauty. She wanted it to reflect the love she and George had shared for art, and for each other. The Joslyn Memorial opened its doors on November 29, 1931. Built in a striking Art Deco style and clad in elegant marble, it housed art galleries, a concert hall, a lecture hall, a library, classrooms, and a serene fountain court. It was, and remains, a gift of pure generosity to the people of Omaha. There is one detail about the opening that captures Sarah Joslyn's character perfectly. Though tens of thousands of people turned out to see the new museum, Sarah insisted there be no fanfare, no speeches, and no ribbon cutting. She did not want the attention. In fact, on the day her extraordinary gift opened to the public, Sarah Joslyn quietly watched from the street, content simply to see her city receive what she and George had dreamed of giving it. It is hard to imagine a more humble or moving way to give. In the decades since, the Joslyn has only grown, expanding several times to make room for more art and more visitors, with its most recent expansion completed in 2024. Yet through every change, it has remained true to Sarah's original purpose: a place of beauty, freely shared, for the good of the whole community. Generations of Omaha schoolchildren have taken their first wide-eyed walk through its galleries, and countless families have spent unhurried afternoons among its treasures. This is exactly why the Joslyn Art Museum is a must-visit when you come to Omaha. It is a place where you can stand inside one woman's love for her husband and her city, surrounded by world-class art in a building that is itself a work of art. Bring the family, take your time, and let the kids discover something wonderful. When you plan your trip to Omaha, make room for the Joslyn, and for the many other treasures this surprising city has to offer. See what is happening around town on our Explore Omaha event calendar, and start planning a visit built around the museum that began as a widow's loving gift.
articles.excerpt: One of the most beautiful buildings in Omaha was a widow's gift to her city in memory of the husband she loved. Here is the moving story of Sarah Joslyn and the Joslyn Art Museum, and why it belongs on your Omaha itinerary.

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: How Omaha Became the Home of College Baseball
articles.body: Every June, while much of the country is firing up the grill and heading to the lake, the eyes of the entire baseball world turn to one place: Omaha, Nebraska. For two unforgettable weeks each summer, the best college baseball teams in America converge on the city for the Men's College World Series, and Omaha transforms into the undisputed capital of the sport. What many visitors do not realize, as they fill the stadium and pack the surrounding streets, is that this beloved tradition almost never happened here at all. The College World Series did not begin in Omaha. The very first one was played in 1947 in Kalamazoo, Michigan, and after another year there it moved to Wichita, Kansas. It was a young, money-losing event still searching for a permanent home. But a group of determined Omaha boosters saw something everyone else had missed. They traveled to the 1949 series in Wichita, made their pitch to NCAA officials, and convinced them to give their city a chance. In 1950, the College World Series came to Omaha for the first time, and it never left. It would be a lovely story if success had been instant, but it was not. For much of its first decade in Omaha, the tournament actually lost money, year after year. A lesser city might have given up. Omaha did the opposite. The community rallied around the event, volunteers poured in their time, local leaders refused to let it fail, and slowly the College World Series put down roots so deep that today it is almost impossible to imagine it anywhere else. That stubborn, hardworking loyalty is about as Omaha as it gets. For sixty seasons, the games were played at the legendary Johnny Rosenblatt Stadium, a ballpark that generations of fans came to love and that still lives warmly in the city's memory. Then, in 2011, the series moved downtown to a gleaming new ballpark, now known as Charles Schwab Field Omaha, just steps from the riverfront and the Old Market. The move marked a new chapter, but the heart of the event never changed. The College World Series remains a family-friendly celebration of young athletes, hometown pride, and the simple joy of summer baseball. What makes the College World Series so special is not only the baseball, though the baseball is wonderful. It is the way the whole city leans in. Neighborhoods fill with fans from across the country. Families make it an annual tradition, passing the love of the games down to their kids. The atmosphere is wholesome, electric, and unmistakably American, the kind of event that reminds you why people fall in love with a sport in the first place. And here is the best part for anyone reading this from out of town: you are absolutely invited. There may be no better time to experience Omaha than in June, when the city is alive with the energy of the College World Series and everything around it. You can catch a game, explore the historic Old Market a short walk away, stroll the beautiful riverfront, and discover a city that knows how to throw a party while keeping it family-friendly. So whether you are a die-hard baseball fan or simply looking for a memorable summer trip, consider putting Omaha on your list. Come see the home of college baseball for yourself, walk the streets that fought to make this tradition theirs, and find out why so many families return year after year. Before you plan your visit, take a look at everything happening around town on our Explore Omaha event calendar, and start mapping out your trip to the city that became the home of college baseball.
articles.excerpt: Every June, the eyes of the baseball world turn to Omaha, Nebraska for the College World Series. But the city had to fight to land it, and almost lost money keeping it. Here is how Omaha became the beloved home of college baseball, and why you should plan a summer visit.

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: Memorial Park: From a Golf Course to Omaha's Front Yard
articles.body: Drive down Dodge Street on the west side of Omaha and you will pass a wide, gently sloping hillside of perfect green lawn, crowned by a dignified stone monument and framed by mature trees. On any pleasant day you will see families spread out on blankets, joggers on the paths, and children rolling down the grass. This is Memorial Park, one of Omaha's most cherished public spaces, often called the city's front yard. But like so much of Omaha, the peaceful scene hides a surprising history. The land that is now Memorial Park was not always a park at all. In its earlier life, this rolling ground had served as a golf course, a private green space on the growing edge of the city. As Omaha expanded in the first half of the twentieth century, civic leaders began to dream of turning it into something that belonged to everyone, a permanent tribute to the men and women who had served and sacrificed in America's wars. That dream became reality in 1948, when Memorial Park was officially dedicated to honor the nation's veterans. And this was no quiet, small-town ribbon cutting. The dedication drew a sitting President of the United States, Harry S. Truman, who came to Omaha to deliver the dedication address. It is a remarkable thing to stand on that hillside today and realize that a president once stood there too, helping a grateful city consecrate the ground to the memory of those who gave so much. In the decades since, Memorial Park has only grown more beloved. Its handsome memorial and colonnade remain a place of quiet reflection, especially meaningful on Memorial Day and Veterans Day, when Omahans gather to remember. But the park is far from solemn the rest of the year. It has become one of the city's favorite gathering spots, a place for picnics, frisbee, evening walks, and simply soaking up a beautiful day. Perhaps the park's most famous modern tradition is its huge free summer concert, when tens of thousands of people blanket the hillside for an evening of music capped by fireworks. It is one of those quintessential Omaha experiences, a whole community spread across the grass under a summer sky, generations of families side by side, in a park built to honor sacrifice and made to be enjoyed in freedom. That is really the magic of Memorial Park. It manages to be two things at once: a solemn tribute and a joyful gathering place, a monument to those who served and a living, breathing piece of everyday Omaha life. It captures something true about the city itself, a place that takes its history and its gratitude seriously while still knowing how to enjoy a beautiful afternoon. If you are planning a visit to Omaha, Memorial Park belongs on your list, whether you come to walk the peaceful grounds, pay your respects at the memorial, or join the crowd for a summer concert on the lawn. It is free, it is beautiful, and it is pure Omaha. Before you come, see what else is happening around the city on our Explore Omaha event calendar, and start planning a trip to the city's front yard and everything around it.
articles.excerpt: One of Omaha's most beloved green spaces was once a golf course, and its dedication in 1948 drew a sitting president of the United States. Here is the story of Memorial Park, and why this peaceful hillside is a must-visit when you come to Omaha.
