articles.title: What to Do When a Debt Collector Calls You
articles.body: A call from a debt collector is one of the most unsettling experiences a family can go through. The phone rings, and suddenly someone on the other end is demanding money, using pressure tactics, and making you feel like you have no options. For many people, the instinct is either to panic and agree to pay whatever they ask, or to ignore the calls entirely and hope they stop. Both of those responses are mistakes. The truth is that you have significant legal rights when it comes to debt collection, and knowing them can change the entire conversation. The Law That Protects You The Fair Debt Collection Practices Act, or FDCPA, is a federal law that sets the rules for how debt collectors can and cannot behave. It applies to third-party debt collectors, meaning companies that collect debts on behalf of others, not to the original creditor collecting their own debt. Under this law, debt collectors are prohibited from using abusive, unfair, or deceptive practices to collect from you. If a collector violates these rules, you have the right to sue them, and they can be required to pay you damages. What a Debt Collector Cannot Do Understanding the specific prohibitions under the FDCPA is the first step in protecting yourself. A debt collector cannot call you before 8 in the morning or after 9 at night unless you give them permission. They cannot call you at work if you tell them your employer does not allow personal calls. They cannot use profanity, make threats of violence, or publish your name on a list of people who owe money. They cannot lie about the amount you owe, falsely claim to be an attorney, or threaten you with arrest. In the United States, you cannot be jailed for failing to pay a civil debt, and any collector who says otherwise is breaking the law. They also cannot contact third parties about your debt. They can call a family member or neighbor once, and only to get your contact information. They cannot tell those people that you owe money. What to Do When the Call Comes When a debt collector calls, the most important thing is to stay calm and not make any commitments on the spot. Do not agree to pay anything, do not acknowledge the debt as yours, and do not give out your bank account or credit card information. You are not obligated to make a payment during that first call, no matter how much pressure they apply. Ask for the collector's name, the name of their company, their mailing address, and the phone number. Ask for the name of the original creditor and the amount they say you owe. Write all of this down. Then tell them you need to verify the debt and that you will contact them in writing. End the call. Request Debt Validation in Writing Within 30 days of the first contact, you have the right to request debt validation. This means you send a written letter asking the collector to prove that the debt is legitimate, that they have the legal right to collect it, and that the amount is correct. Send this letter by certified mail with a return receipt so you have proof they received it. While the collector is validating the debt, they are required to stop all collection efforts. If they cannot validate the debt, they cannot continue to collect it. Many debts are sold and resold, and records get lost. It is not uncommon for a collector to be unable to validate a debt, especially if it is old. If that happens, the collection efforts must stop. Know the Statute of Limitations Every state has a statute of limitations on debt, which is the legal time limit for a creditor to sue you. In Nebraska, the statute of limitations on most written contracts is five years. After that time, the debt is still legally owed, but the creditor cannot take you to court to force payment. If a collector threatens to sue you on a debt that is past the statute of limitations, that is a violation of the FDCPA. Be careful here. Making a payment, or even acknowledging the debt in writing, can restart the statute of limitations clock. This is why you should never make a payment or acknowledge a debt without first understanding your rights. When to Get Help If a debt collector is harassing you, calling repeatedly, contacting your family, or making threats, you need legal help. The same is true if you are unsure whether a debt is legitimate, if the amount seems wrong, or if you are being sued. Dealing with debt collection on your own can lead to mistakes that cost you thousands of dollars and stay on your credit report for years. A LegalShield membership gives you access to attorney consultation for covered debt collection matters. Instead of guessing about your rights, you can get clear guidance from someone who knows the law and can help you respond appropriately. You can learn more at this link. The Bottom Line A debt collector's call is not the end of the world, and you are not powerless. Know your rights under the FDCPA, do not make commitments on the spot, request debt validation in writing, and understand the statute of limitations. Most importantly, get help when the situation is more than you can handle alone. Protecting your family's financial future starts with knowing what to do when that call comes. --- 👉 Get Protected with LegalShield →. To learn more: click HERE.
articles.excerpt: A call from a debt collector can be intimidating, but you have more rights than you think. Here is exactly what to do, what to say, and what to never say when that call comes.

articles.title: Your Rights When You Are Fired or Laid Off
articles.body: Losing your job is one of the most stressful experiences a family can go through. Whether it comes as a surprise or you see it coming, the moment you are told your employment is ending, your mind floods with questions. How will you pay the mortgage? What about health insurance? Did they have the right to do this? The fear is real, but so are your rights. Knowing what the law says can help you protect your family and make smart decisions during a difficult time. Nebraska Is an At-Will Employment State The first thing to understand is that Nebraska, like most states, follows the doctrine of at-will employment. This means that either the employer or the employee can end the employment relationship at any time, for almost any reason, or for no reason at all. Your employer does not need a good reason to fire you. They do not need to give you warning. They do not need to prove that you did something wrong. But there are important limits to at-will employment, and those limits are where your rights begin. When a Firing Is Illegal Even in an at-will state, there are reasons for which an employer cannot legally fire you. These are called exceptions to at-will employment, and they fall into several categories. First, an employer cannot fire you based on a protected characteristic. Federal law prohibits termination because of race, color, religion, sex, national origin, age, or disability. Nebraska law adds additional protections. If you believe you were fired because of one of these characteristics, you may have a wrongful termination claim. Second, an employer cannot fire you in retaliation for exercising a legal right. If you filed a workers' compensation claim, reported discrimination, took legally protected leave, or refused to do something illegal, and were fired shortly after, that may be retaliation. Third, an employer cannot fire you in a way that violates an employment contract. If you have a written contract that specifies the terms and duration of your employment, or if your employer's handbook creates implied contractual obligations, those terms must be followed. Finally, Nebraska recognizes a public policy exception. An employer cannot fire you for doing something that public policy encourages, such as serving on a jury or reporting a safety violation, or for refusing to do something that public policy discourages, such as committing perjury. Your Right to Final Wages When your employment ends, you have the right to receive your final paycheck. In Nebraska, if you are fired, your employer must pay you on the next regular payday. If you quit, your employer must pay you on the next regular payday or within two weeks, whichever is earlier. This includes all earned wages, including any accrued vacation pay that your employer's policy says is payable at termination. If your employer fails to pay your final wages on time, you may be entitled to recover the wages plus a penalty. Do not let this go. Unpaid wages are money your family needs. Unemployment Insurance Benefits If you lose your job through no fault of your own, you may be eligible for unemployment insurance benefits through the Nebraska Department of Labor. These benefits provide temporary financial assistance while you look for new work. To qualify, you must have earned enough wages during your base period, be able and available to work, and be actively seeking employment. If you were fired for cause, your employer may contest your unemployment claim. But the standard for disqualification is specific. You must have been fired for deliberate misconduct connected to your work, not simply for poor performance or a mistake. If your employer says you were fired for cause but the facts do not support it, you have the right to appeal and present your side at a hearing. Severance Agreements: Read Before You Sign If your employer offers you a severance package, read it carefully before signing. A severance agreement is a legal contract, and in exchange for the money or benefits being offered, you are typically being asked to give up certain rights. Most severance agreements include a release of claims, meaning you agree not to sue the employer for anything related to your employment or termination. They may also include non-compete clauses, non-disparagement clauses, and confidentiality provisions. Do not sign a severance agreement on the spot. Take it home, read every word, and get legal advice. You may have room to negotiate, and you need to understand exactly what rights you are giving up before you agree to anything. When to Get Help If you believe you were wrongfully terminated, if your employer is contesting your unemployment benefits unfairly, if your final wages have not been paid, or if you have been offered a severance agreement you do not fully understand, you need legal help. Employment law is complex, and the deadlines for filing claims are strict. Missing a deadline can mean losing your right to pursue a claim entirely. A LegalShield membership gives you access to attorney consultation for covered employment matters, including severance agreement review. Instead of navigating this alone, you can get clear guidance from someone who knows the law. You can learn more at this link. The Bottom Line Losing your job is hard, but you are not without rights. Understand at-will employment, know when a firing is illegal, collect your final wages, apply for unemployment, and never sign a severance agreement without understanding it. Most importantly, get help when you need it. Your family's future is worth protecting, and knowing your rights is the first step. --- 👉 Get Protected with LegalShield →. To learn more: click HERE.

articles.title: What a Demand Letter Actually Does and When to Use One
articles.body: Most people think that legal disputes start with a lawsuit. They imagine a courtroom, a judge, and a long and expensive process. But the reality is that many legal disputes are resolved before a lawsuit is ever filed, and one of the most effective tools for doing that is something called a demand letter. It is exactly what it sounds like: a formal letter that demands something from another party, usually payment or action, and lays out the legal basis for that demand. It is simple, it is powerful, and most families never use it because they do not know it exists. What a Demand Letter Actually Does A demand letter serves several important purposes. First, it puts the other party on formal notice that you have a claim against them. It tells them exactly what the dispute is about, what you believe you are owed, and what will happen if they do not respond. This eliminates any claim they might later make that they did not know there was a problem. Second, it creates a paper trail. If the dispute eventually ends up in court, the demand letter is evidence that you tried to resolve the matter in good faith before filing a lawsuit. Judges look favorably on parties who attempted to resolve disputes without burdening the court system, and a well-written demand letter shows that you did exactly that. Third, and perhaps most importantly, it often works. Many disputes are resolved by a demand letter alone. The other party reads it, realizes you are serious, understands that you know your rights, and decides it is easier and cheaper to settle the matter than to fight it out in court. When to Send a Demand Letter Demand letters are appropriate in a wide range of situations. If a contractor did poor work and refuses to fix it, a demand letter can require them to either complete the work properly or refund your money. If someone damaged your property and will not pay for the repairs, a demand letter can lay out the cost and demand payment. If you were sold a defective product and the seller will not honor a warranty, a demand letter can demand a refund or replacement. If a landlord refuses to return a security deposit without valid reason, a demand letter can require its return. The common thread is that a demand letter is appropriate when you have a legitimate claim, the other party is not responding or is refusing to do the right thing, and you want to resolve the matter without going to court if possible. What a Good Demand Letter Contains A demand letter does not need to be long or filled with legal jargon. In fact, the best demand letters are clear, concise, and written in plain language. Here is what every effective demand letter should include. The facts. Start by describing what happened, in chronological order, with specific dates, amounts, and details. Stick to the facts and avoid emotional language. The goal is to be clear and credible, not to vent. The legal basis. Explain why you believe the other party is legally obligated to you. You do not need to cite specific statutes, but you should explain the reasoning. For example, "Under our written agreement, you were obligated to complete the work by June 1. The work was not completed, and the defects are documented in the attached inspection report." The demand. State exactly what you want. Be specific. If you want a refund, state the amount. If you want work completed, describe what needs to be done. Do not leave it vague. The deadline. Give the other party a specific deadline to respond, usually 10 to 14 days from the date of the letter. This creates urgency and shows you are serious. The consequence. State what will happen if they do not comply. Usually, this means you will pursue legal action. You do not need to threaten or be aggressive. A simple statement that you will "pursue all available legal remedies" is sufficient. The method of delivery. Send the letter by certified mail with a return receipt so you have proof it was received. Keep a copy of the letter and the receipt. What Happens After You Send It After you send a demand letter, one of several things will happen. The other party may comply with your demand, which is the best outcome. They may respond with a counteroffer, opening a negotiation. They may ignore the letter entirely, which strengthens your position if you later file a lawsuit. Or they may respond with a denial, which at least clarifies the dispute and moves things forward. In many cases, the mere act of sending a formal, well-written demand letter is enough to shift the dynamic. The other party realizes you are not going away, that you understand your rights, and that ignoring you will have consequences. When to Get Help Writing One You can write a demand letter yourself, and many people do. But there is a significant advantage to having a letter that comes from, or is reviewed by, an attorney. A letter on attorney letterhead carries weight that a personal letter does not. It signals to the other party that you have legal representation and are prepared to use it. It also ensures that your legal reasoning is sound and that you are not accidentally giving up rights or making claims that will not hold up. A LegalShield membership includes the review of certain legal documents and access to attorney consultation, which means you can have a professional help you craft or review a demand letter for covered matters without paying hundreds of dollars in hourly fees. You can learn more at this link. The Bottom Line A demand letter is one of the most effective and underused tools available to regular families. It puts the other party on notice, creates a paper trail, and often resolves disputes without a lawsuit ever being filed. If you are in a dispute where someone owes you money or has failed to honor an obligation, do not just stew in frustration. Put it in writing, be clear about what you want, and give them a deadline. And if you need help, get it. Knowing how to use a demand letter is a skill that can save your family time, money, and stress. --- 👉 Get Protected with LegalShield →. To learn more: click HERE.

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: Your Rights as a Consumer When a Product Injures You
articles.body: We buy products every day without thinking twice. A kitchen appliance, a child's toy, a piece of furniture, a household tool. We trust that the things we bring into our homes are safe, because there are laws and standards that are supposed to ensure they are. But sometimes, a product is not safe. Sometimes it has a design flaw, a manufacturing defect, or a missing warning, and someone gets hurt. When that happens to you or someone in your family, the law gives you rights. Understanding those rights can make the difference between bearing the burden alone and holding the responsible party accountable. What Product Liability Means Product liability is the area of law that holds manufacturers, distributors, and sellers responsible for injuries caused by defective products. The core idea is simple: if a company puts a product into the stream of commerce, and that product is unreasonably dangerous because of a defect, the company can be held legally responsible for the harm it causes. You do not have to prove that the manufacturer was careless in the traditional sense. In many cases, product liability operates under a theory called strict liability, which means that if the product was defective and that defect caused your injury, the manufacturer is responsible regardless of whether they intended any harm or took reasonable care. This is different from most other areas of personal injury law, where you typically have to prove negligence. The Three Types of Product Defects Product liability claims generally fall into three categories, and understanding which one applies to your situation is important. The first is a design defect. This means the product was inherently dangerous because of how it was designed, not because of a mistake in manufacturing. Every unit of the product has the same flaw. An example would be a space heater designed in a way that makes it prone to tipping over and starting fires. The second is a manufacturing defect. This means the design was fine, but something went wrong during the manufacturing process that made this particular product dangerous. Not every unit has the problem, only the defective ones. An example would be a batch of medication that was contaminated during production. The third is a failure to warn, also called a marketing defect. This means the product was designed and manufactured correctly, but it did not include adequate warnings or instructions about a known danger. An example would be a cleaning chemical that can produce dangerous fumes when mixed with another common household product, but the label did not warn about it. Who Can Be Held Responsible One of the important things to understand about product liability is that multiple parties in the chain of commerce can potentially be held responsible. The manufacturer of the product, the manufacturer of a component part, the company that assembled or installed the product, the wholesaler or distributor, and the retail store that sold the product to you can all potentially be part of a product liability claim. This matters because sometimes the manufacturer is a large overseas company that is difficult to sue directly. In those cases, the domestic distributor or retailer may be the party you can hold accountable. The key is that the product was sold, it was defective, and the defect caused your injury. What to Do If a Product Injures You or a Family Member If a product causes an injury, take the following steps. First, seek medical attention immediately. Your health and the health of your family come first, and the medical records will also serve as important evidence of the injury and its cause. Second, preserve the product and everything that came with it. Do not throw the product away, do not try to fix it, and do not return it to the store. Keep it in the exact condition it was in when the injury occurred, along with the packaging, the instructions, the receipt, and any other materials that came with it. This is critical evidence. Third, document everything. Take photographs of the product, the injury, and the scene where it happened. Write down exactly what occurred, including the date, the time, and the sequence of events. If there were witnesses, get their names and contact information. Fourth, report the incident. You can file a report with the Consumer Product Safety Commission at SaferProducts.gov. This creates an official record and can help prevent others from being injured by the same product. Fifth, do not contact the manufacturer or accept any settlement offer before you have spoken with an attorney. Manufacturers and their insurance companies may try to resolve the matter quickly and quietly, offering you a small payment in exchange for signing away your rights. Do not sign anything. The Time Limit to File a Claim Every state has a statute of limitations for product liability claims, which is the legal deadline for filing a lawsuit. In Nebraska, the statute of limitations for personal injury claims, including product liability, is generally four years from the date of the injury. But there are exceptions, and the clock can start at different times depending on the circumstances. If you wait too long, you lose your right to pursue a claim entirely, no matter how strong your case is. When to Get Help Product liability cases are complex. They often require expert testimony, technical analysis of the product, and an understanding of both state and federal law. They are not something you should try to handle on your own. If you or a family member has been injured by a product, you need legal help to understand your rights and pursue them effectively. A LegalShield membership gives you access to attorney consultation, and for covered personal injury matters, can help you understand whether you have a claim and what your options are. You can learn more at this link. The Bottom Line When a product injures you or someone you love, the law is on your side. Product liability holds manufacturers and sellers responsible for the harm caused by defective products, and you have the right to seek compensation for medical bills, lost wages, pain, and suffering. Preserve the evidence, document everything, report the incident, and get legal help before you talk to the manufacturer. Your family deserves to be protected, and knowing your rights is the first step. --- 👉 Get Protected with LegalShield →. To learn more: click HERE.
articles.excerpt: When a product you bought hurts you or someone in your family, the law gives you real rights. Here is what product liability means, who is responsible, and what to do if it happens.

articles.title: Your Rights During a Traffic Stop in Nebraska
articles.body: Being pulled over is one of the most common ways that regular, law-abiding people come into contact with law enforcement. It can happen to anyone, and it is almost always stressful. Your heart rate goes up, your mind races, and it is easy to say or do something you later regret. The best way to handle a traffic stop is to know your rights ahead of time, stay calm, and make smart decisions in the moment. This is not about being difficult or confrontational. It is about knowing what the law says so that you can protect yourself and your family while still being respectful and cooperative. What to Do When You See the Lights When you see flashing lights behind you, pull over to the right side of the road as safely and quickly as you can. Turn off your engine, roll down your driver side window, and put your hands on the steering wheel where the officer can see them. If it is dark, turn on your interior light. These are not legal requirements, but they are practical steps that help the officer feel safe, and a calm officer makes for a smoother stop. Wait for the officer to approach before you start reaching for anything. Your license, registration, and proof of insurance are what you will need, but do not go digging for them until the officer asks. Sudden movements can be misinterpreted. What You Are Required to Do In Nebraska, you are required to show your driver's license, vehicle registration, and proof of insurance when asked by a law enforcement officer during a traffic stop. You are also required to step out of the vehicle if the officer asks you to. These are not optional. Refusing to comply with these basic requests will only make the situation worse and can lead to additional charges. What You Are Not Required to Do Here is where many people get confused. You are not required to answer questions beyond providing your identification and vehicle documents. If the officer asks where you are going, where you are coming from, or whether you know why you were pulled over, you have the right to remain silent. You can politely say, "I prefer not to answer questions without an attorney present." You do not have to consent to a search of your vehicle. If the officer asks to search your car, you can say no. Be polite but firm. Say, "I do not consent to a search of my vehicle." If the officer searches anyway, do not resist physically. The fact that you did not consent will matter later if the case goes to court. Can the Officer Search Your Car Without Your Permission? In general, an officer needs one of three things to search your vehicle without your consent: probable cause, a search warrant, or a lawful arrest. Probable cause means the officer has a specific, articulable reason to believe that evidence of a crime is in the car. This could be the smell of something illegal, something visible in plain view, or a drug-sniffing dog alerting. If none of those apply and you do not give consent, the search may be illegal, and any evidence found could potentially be thrown out in court. What About Passengers? If you have family members in the car, they also have rights. Passengers are generally free to ask if they are being detained and, if not, whether they can leave. In practice, during a traffic stop, it is usually best for everyone to remain calm, keep their hands visible, and follow instructions. If an officer asks a passenger for identification, Nebraska law does not require passengers to provide ID unless they are being lawfully detained or arrested, but refusing can prolong the stop. What to Do If You Feel Your Rights Were Violated Do not argue with the officer on the side of the road. That is not the place to make your case. If you believe your rights were violated, the time to address it is afterward. Write down everything you remember as soon as you get home: the time, the location, the officer's name and badge number, what was said, and what happened. If you have a dash camera, save the footage. If you received a citation, you have the right to contest it in court. If you believe the stop itself was unlawful, that is a legal issue that may require professional help to address properly. This is where having access to legal counsel ahead of time is valuable. Traffic stops can lead to charges that affect your license, your insurance rates, and even your criminal record. A LegalShield membership gives you access to attorney consultation for covered traffic matters, so you can get advice before you make decisions that could follow you for years. You can learn more at this link. The Bottom Line A traffic stop does not have to be a crisis. Know what you are required to do, know what you are not required to do, stay calm, and be respectful. If something goes wrong, handle it afterward with the right help. The goal is to get home safely and protect your family, and knowing your rights is the first step. --- 👉 Get Protected with LegalShield →. To learn more: click HERE.
articles.excerpt: A traffic stop can be stressful, but knowing your rights ahead of time helps you stay calm and protect yourself and your family. Here is what every Nebraska driver should understand.

articles.title: What Every Renter in Omaha Needs to Know
articles.body: Renting a home is the reality for many families in Omaha, and for good reason. It offers flexibility, lower upfront costs, and freedom from the responsibilities of homeownership. But renting also comes with a set of legal rights and responsibilities that many tenants do not fully understand. Whether you are signing your first lease or you have been renting for years, knowing what the law says can help you avoid costly mistakes and protect your family. Nebraska's Landlord and Tenant Act In Nebraska, the relationship between landlords and tenants is governed by the Nebraska Residential Landlord and Tenant Act. This law sets out the rights and responsibilities of both parties and applies to most rental agreements in the state. Even if your lease says something different, the law takes precedence. A lease cannot take away rights that the law gives you. Your Right to a Habitable Home The most important right you have as a tenant is the right to a livable, safe, and habitable home. This is called the implied warranty of habitability, and it means your landlord is legally required to maintain the property in a condition that is fit for human habitation. This includes working plumbing, safe electrical systems, adequate heating, and a structure that is sound and weatherproofed. If something essential breaks, like your heat in January or your water supply, your landlord is responsible for fixing it. You should report the problem in writing and keep a copy. If the landlord does not respond, you have legal options, including the right to repair and deduct the cost from your rent under certain conditions, or in serious cases, the right to terminate the lease. Security Deposits: What You Need to Know In Nebraska, a landlord can charge a security deposit, but there are rules. The deposit cannot exceed one month's rent for unfurnished units, or one and a half months' rent for furnished units. When you move out, the landlord has 14 days to return your deposit or provide a written itemized list of deductions. If the landlord fails to do so, you may be entitled to recover the full deposit. Before you move in, document the condition of the unit. Take photos of every room, note any existing damage, and make sure the landlord has a copy. When you move out, do the same thing. This protects you from being charged for damage you did not cause. Your Right to Privacy Your landlord cannot enter your home whenever they want. Under Nebraska law, the landlord must give you reasonable notice, typically at least 24 hours, before entering the unit, except in emergencies. Your home is your home, even if you do not own it. The landlord's right to access the property does not override your right to quiet enjoyment of the space. Eviction: What the Law Requires A landlord cannot simply change the locks or force you out. In Nebraska, eviction is a legal process that requires the landlord to give you proper notice and, if you do not leave, to file an eviction action in court. You have the right to receive notice of the eviction, the right to a hearing, and the right to present your side of the story before a judge. The type of notice required depends on the reason for the eviction. For nonpayment of rent, the landlord must give you a seven-day notice to pay or quit. For lease violations, the notice period may be longer. If you receive an eviction notice, do not ignore it. You have a limited time to respond, and missing that window can result in a default judgment against you. Lease Agreements: Read Before You Sign Before you sign a lease, read every word. Look for clauses about late fees, maintenance responsibilities, pet policies, early termination, and what happens at the end of the lease term. If you do not understand something, ask. If a clause seems unfair or unclear, that is a sign you need to get advice before signing. Many tenants sign leases without fully understanding what they are agreeing to, and that leads to problems later. A lease is a binding legal contract, and the terms matter. When to Get Help If you are dealing with a landlord who will not make repairs, who is trying to keep your deposit unfairly, who is entering your home without notice, or who is trying to evict you without following the law, you may need legal help. The problem is that most renters do not have an attorney on call, and by the time they realize they need one, the situation has already escalated. This is where having a legal plan in place ahead of time is valuable. A LegalShield membership gives you access to attorney consultation and document review for covered matters, including lease review and landlord-tenant disputes. Instead of guessing about your rights, you can get a clear answer from someone who knows the law. You can learn more at this link. The Bottom Line As a renter in Omaha, you have real legal rights. You have the right to a safe and habitable home, the right to privacy, the right to a fair return of your security deposit, and the right to due process in an eviction. Know these rights, document everything, and get help when you need it. Your home is where your family lives, and you deserve to have it protected. --- 👉 Get Protected with LegalShield →. articles.title: What to Do When You Get a Letter from the IRS
articles.body: Getting a letter from the IRS is one of those moments that can make your stomach drop. You see the official envelope, and your mind immediately goes to the worst-case scenario. But here is the truth: most IRS letters are not audits. They are often simple notices about a missing signature, a math error, or a balance that needs attention. The key is knowing what to do next, and doing it calmly. Step One: Do Not Panic The first thing to understand is that an IRS letter is not a summons to court. It is a notice, and notices have a process. The IRS sends out millions of these letters every year, and the vast majority are resolved without anything dramatic happening. Take a deep breath, open the envelope, and read every word carefully. Do not ignore it. Ignoring an IRS letter is the only way a small problem becomes a big one. Step Two: Identify What the Letter Is Actually Saying Every IRS notice has a letter number, usually printed at the top right corner. It will look something like CP2000, CP504, or Letter 2205. That number tells you exactly what the letter is about. A CP2000, for example, means the IRS thinks there is a mismatch between what you reported and what your employer or bank reported. A CP504 means you have an unpaid balance and the IRS is warning you about possible collection action. Write down the notice number, the tax year it references, and the response deadline. Step Three: Compare It to Your Records Before you respond to anything, pull out your tax return for the year in question. Compare the numbers on the letter to the numbers on your return. If the IRS says you forgot to report income, check your W-2s and 1099s. If they say you owe more, look at the math. Sometimes the IRS is wrong. Sometimes you made an honest mistake. Either way, you need to know which one it is before you respond. Step Four: Respond Before the Deadline Every IRS letter has a response deadline, usually 30 or 60 days from the date of the letter. That deadline is real. If you miss it, the IRS will assume their version of the facts is correct and move forward accordingly. If you agree with what the letter says, follow the instructions to pay or set up a payment plan. If you disagree, you have the right to respond in writing with your explanation and supporting documents. Keep a copy of everything you send. Step Five: Get Help If the Numbers Are Big If the letter is about a small amount, you can probably handle it yourself. But if the IRS is claiming you owe thousands of dollars, or if the letter mentions an audit or a proposed adjustment that could significantly change your tax liability, you need professional help. This is where many families get in trouble. They try to handle a complex tax issue on their own, say the wrong thing, and end up owing more than they should have. The problem is that hiring a tax attorney or CPA for an IRS dispute can cost hundreds or even thousands of dollars up front. For most families, that is not money they have sitting around. This is exactly the kind of situation where having a legal plan in place ahead of time makes all the difference. Plan Ahead Before the Letter Arrives The best time to figure out how you would handle an IRS letter is before you ever receive one. Having access to legal guidance means that when the letter does show up, you are not making decisions out of fear. You can pick up the phone, talk to someone who knows the tax code, and get a clear answer about what to do next. If you do not already have a plan for situations like this, it is worth looking into. A LegalShield membership gives your family access to legal consultation, document review, and tax audit defense services for a flat monthly rate that is far less than what a single hour with a tax attorney would cost. You can learn more and sign up at this link. The Bottom Line An IRS letter is not the end of the world. It is a notice with a process. Read it carefully, compare it to your records, respond before the deadline, and get help if the stakes are high. Most importantly, have a plan in place before the letter ever arrives. That way, when it does, you are ready. --- 👉 Get Protected with LegalShield →. To learn more: click HERE.

articles.title: How to Protect Your Family from Identity Theft
articles.body: Identity theft is not something that only happens to other people. In 2024, the Federal Trade Commission received over 1.2 million reports of identity theft, and that number is climbing every year. Thieves do not need to steal your wallet anymore. They need your name, your Social Security number, and a few details they can find online, and suddenly they are opening credit cards, filing tax returns, or even taking out loans in your name. For a family, the damage can be devastating, and undoing it can take months. The good news is that most identity theft is preventable if you know what to watch for and take a few simple steps ahead of time. Understand How Thieves Get Your Information Before you can protect yourself, it helps to know how the theft actually happens. The most common methods are data breaches, phishing emails, stolen mail, and lost or stolen wallets. A data breach at a company you do business with can expose your information without you doing anything wrong. A phishing email tricks you into clicking a link and entering your password on a fake website. Stolen mail can give a thief access to bank statements, credit card offers, and tax documents. The key is to be aware that your information is out there, and to take steps to make it harder for someone to use it even if they get it. Freeze Your Credit This is the single most effective thing you can do to protect your family from identity theft. A credit freeze restricts access to your credit report, which means that even if a thief has your Social Security number, they cannot open new accounts in your name because lenders cannot check your credit. You can freeze and unfreeze your credit for free at all three major credit bureaus: Equifax, Experian, and TransUnion. It takes about 15 minutes to set up, and it is one of the strongest defenses available. If you need to apply for credit, you can temporarily lift the freeze. When you are done, you put it back. There is no downside to having your credit frozen when you are not actively applying for new accounts. Protect Your Children's Identities Here is something many parents do not think about: children are prime targets for identity theft because their Social Security numbers are clean and unused. A thief who gets a child's Social Security number can open accounts and run up debt for years before anyone notices, because no one checks a child's credit report. If your child starts receiving credit card offers in the mail, or if you get a notice from the IRS about income your child supposedly earned, that is a red flag. You can freeze your child's credit at all three bureaus as well. Be Smart About Passwords and Online Accounts Use a different password for every important account, and use a password manager to keep track of them. Enable two-factor authentication on your email, bank, and credit accounts. Be cautious about what you share on social media, especially details like your full birth date, your address, or your mother's maiden name, all of which are commonly used as security questions. Watch Your Mail and Your Statements Shred any documents with personal information before you throw them away. Pick up your mail promptly, and if you are going to be out of town, have someone collect it or put a hold on delivery. Review your bank and credit card statements every month for charges you do not recognize. A small unauthorized charge is often a test before a larger theft. Know What to Do If It Happens If you discover that your identity has been stolen, act fast. Contact the fraud departments at any affected financial institutions. Place a fraud alert on your credit reports at all three bureaus. File a report with the Federal Trade Commission at IdentityTheft.gov, and file a report with your local police department. The FTC report will serve as proof that you are a victim, which you will need when disputing fraudulent charges. The hard part is not the initial response. The hard part is the months of follow-up, the phone calls, the letters, and the time spent making sure every fraudulent account is closed and every credit report is corrected. This is where having legal support makes a real difference. A LegalShield membership includes identity theft consultation and restoration services, meaning you have professionals who can help guide you through the process of reclaiming your identity. You can learn more at this link. The Bottom Line Identity theft is a real threat, but it is a manageable one. Freeze your credit, protect your children's identities, be smart online, and watch your mail and statements. Most importantly, have a plan in place before something goes wrong. The time to figure out how to protect your family is now, not after the damage is done. --- 👉 Get Protected with LegalShield →. To learn more: click HERE.
articles.excerpt: Identity theft can happen to anyone, and the damage can take months to undo. Here are practical steps every family should take to protect their personal information and what to do if the worst happens.

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: What Happens to Your Family If You Die Without a Will
articles.excerpt: Most families don't have a will. Most families think they'll get to it eventually. Here's what actually happens when they don't.
articles.body: ## What Happens to Your Family If You Die Without a Will Most people know they should have a will. Most people don't have one. And most people believe — somewhere in the back of their mind — that *their* family will be fine. That their spouse will get everything. That their kids will be taken care of. That the people they love will figure it out. Here's what actually happens. --- ## 1. What "Intestate" Actually Means When you die without a will, the law has a word for that: **intestate**. It means the state — not you — gets to decide what happens to everything you own and everyone you love. Every state has intestacy laws: a preset formula that determines who gets your assets, who raises your kids, and who manages your estate. These laws were written for the average family. They don't know your family. They don't know your wishes. They don't care that you always intended to leave your mother's ring to your daughter or that your brother-in-law is the last person on earth you'd want near your finances. When there's no will, the state's formula wins. Every time. --- ## 2. What Happens to Your Home This is where families get blindsided the most. If you own your home and you die without a will, what happens next depends on how the property is titled. If you and your spouse own it jointly with right of survivorship, your spouse inherits it automatically. That part works fine. But if the title is in your name only — or if you're unmarried — the home goes through **probate court**. That process can take months. Sometimes years. And it costs money: court fees, attorney fees, and in some cases, forced sale if the heirs can't agree. Your family might still be living in that house while a judge decides who legally owns it. --- ## 3. What Happens to Your Money Bank accounts, retirement accounts, and life insurance policies all have **beneficiary designations** — and those override your will entirely. If you named your spouse as beneficiary on your 401(k), they get it regardless of any other legal situation. But any money sitting in accounts without a designated beneficiary — a checking account, a savings account, a brokerage account — goes into your estate and gets distributed according to intestacy law. In Nebraska, that generally means your spouse gets the first portion, and the rest is divided between your spouse and your children. Not all to your spouse. *Split*. Which means your spouse may not have full access to funds they were counting on — not right away, and not without court involvement. --- ## 4. What Happens to Your Children This is the one that keeps parents up at night — or should. If both parents die without a will, **a judge decides who raises your children.** The judge will look at the most qualified relative who steps forward. They'll consider what's in the best interest of the child. They'll do their best. But they don't know your sister's patience, your best friend's values, or the fact that your own parents are loving but not physically able to raise young kids anymore. They don't know that you and your co-parent had a clear person in mind — you just never wrote it down. A will is the only legal document that lets you name a guardian for your children. Without it, that decision belongs to a courtroom. --- ## 5. The Myth of "We'll Figure It Out" Here is what actually happens when families "figure it out" without a will: Greif turns into disagreement. Disagreement turns into tension. Tension turns into estrangement. A grandmother and a daughter-in-law both believe they're acting in the children's best interest. A brother who helped with the down payment on a house believes he has a claim. A second spouse and children from a first marriage both have legal rights that collide. None of these people are bad. They're just human. And when there's no clear instruction from the person who died, love and loss and money create conflict that courts have to settle. **The people who suffer most in that process are the children.** --- ## 6. What a Basic Estate Plan Actually Includes A complete estate plan isn't as complicated — or as expensive — as most people assume. A solid basic plan includes: - **A will** — names your beneficiaries, names a guardian for your children, names an executor to carry out your wishes - **A durable power of attorney** — names someone to manage your finances if you become incapacitated (not just when you die) - **A healthcare directive / living will** — tells doctors and family what kind of care you want if you can't speak for yourself - **Beneficiary designation review** — making sure your retirement accounts, life insurance, and bank accounts all point to the right people That's it. Four documents. They don't require a complicated trust or a high-net-worth portfolio. They require sitting down with someone who knows what they're doing and actually doing it. If cost has been the barrier, it's worth knowing that legal services memberships — like [LegalShield](https://www.legalshieldassociate.com/pettitenterprises) — offer access to licensed attorneys for a flat monthly fee, including will preparation and estate planning documents. For families who've been putting this off because "lawyers are expensive," that changes the math entirely. --- ## 7. The Honest Cost of Not Having One Probate in Nebraska typically costs between **3% and 7% of the estate's value** in fees and court costs. On a $300,000 estate — a modest home and some savings — that's up to $21,000 paid to the process instead of your family. Add attorney fees for contested matters. Add the time: probate can take 12 to 18 months minimum for complex situations. Add the emotional cost of your family having to fight a legal process while they're grieving. And then add the thing that can't be measured: the guardian your children ended up with not being the one you would have chosen. A will costs a few hundred dollars. A legal services membership costs less than a dinner out each month. The cost of not having one is measured in everything that matters. --- ## You Don't Have to Figure This Out Alone If you've been meaning to get this done and just haven't, you're not alone — and you're not a bad parent or spouse for not having it yet. But "eventually" has a way of never arriving. Pick one thing this week: look up your state's intestacy laws, review your beneficiary designations, or explore whether a legal services plan fits your budget. Your family is worth the conversation. --- *Have questions about estate planning or other legal rights families should know? Drop them in the comments — we read every one.* --- **Ready to protect your family today?** LegalShield gives you access to a real attorney for a low monthly fee — no hourly rates, no intimidating consultations. [👉 Get Protected with LegalShield →](https://allanpettit.legalshieldassociate.com/). To learn more: click HERE.

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: The Conversation Your Marriage Has Been Waiting For
articles.excerpt: Most couples talk every day. But when was the last time you had a conversation that actually mattered? Here's how to start one.
articles.body:
## The Conversation Your Marriage Has Been Waiting For

You talk to your spouse every day.

*"Did you pick up the kids?"* *"What do you want for dinner?"* *"Did you see that email from the school?"*

Logistics. Schedules. The endless coordination of two lives running parallel.

But when was the last time you had a conversation that actually *meant something*?

Not an argument. Not a check-in. A real conversation — the kind where you walked away feeling closer, more known, more seen.

If you're having to think about it... that's the point.

---

## Why We Stop Talking (Really Talking)

It's not that couples stop caring. It's that the conversations that used to come naturally — in the early days, when everything was new — get replaced by the business of running a household.

And over time, without realizing it, you can end up living with your best friend and feeling strangely alone.

This isn't a crisis. It's just drift. And drift can be reversed — not with a therapy session necessarily, but sometimes with a single honest conversation at the right moment.

---

## 10 Questions Worth Asking Your Spouse Tonight

These aren't trick questions. They're not therapy homework. They're just invitations to be known a little better.

Pick one. Light a candle. Put the phones face down. And actually listen to the answer.

**1. What's something you've been wanting to tell me but haven't found the right moment?**

**2. What do I do that makes you feel most loved — and what do I sometimes miss?**

**3. What's a dream you had when you were younger that you've never talked to me about?**

**4. Is there anything in our life right now that's draining you that I might not know about?**

**5. What's something you're proud of yourself for lately?**

**6. If we could completely change one thing about how we spend our time together, what would it be?**

**7. What's something you've always wanted to try but felt too embarrassed to bring up?**

**8. When do you feel most like yourself — and do I make space for that version of you?**

**9. What would "a really good year for us" look like?**

**10. Is there anything you need from me right now that I haven't been giving?**

---

## A Few Ground Rules

These questions only work if you actually *listen* — not to respond, not to defend yourself, but to understand.

If your spouse says something that surprises you or stings a little, your first job isn't to explain yourself. It's to say: *"Tell me more about that."*

The goal isn't to solve anything in one night. The goal is to make your spouse feel safe enough to tell you the truth — and to remind them that you genuinely want to know.

---

## The Smallest Possible Step

You don't need a weekend getaway or a marriage retreat (though those are great if you can do them).

You need twenty minutes. A quiet room. One question.

That's it. That's the whole prescription.

The couples who stay close over decades aren't the ones who never drift — they're the ones who notice when they have, and choose to row back toward each other.

---

## Looking for Something to Do Together This Weekend?

Sometimes the best conversations happen when you're out in the world together — walking through a farmers market, watching the sun set at a park, or experiencing something new side by side.

👉 **[See What's Happening in Omaha This Weekend →](/events)**

We update the calendar every week with family-friendly events, live music, outdoor festivals, and more.

---

*What's a question that changed a conversation in your relationship? Share it below — you might help someone else.*

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: Date Night Is Not Dead — It Just Needs a New Idea
articles.excerpt: You don't have to spend a lot of money or leave the house to have a real date night. You just have to be intentional about it.
articles.body:
## Date Night Is Not Dead — It Just Needs a New Idea

Let's be honest for a second.

Somewhere between the kids, the job, the bills, and the pile of laundry that never seems to go away, date night turned into something you talk about doing but never actually do.

And when you *do* go out, it's the same restaurant you always go to, the same small talk, the same feeling that you're just two people sharing a table instead of a life.

Here's the truth: **date night isn't dead. It just needs a new idea.**

---

## Why Date Night Actually Matters

This isn't just about having fun — though that matters too.

Research on healthy marriages is pretty clear: couples who regularly spend intentional one-on-one time together report higher satisfaction, better communication, and more resilience when hard times come.

"Intentional" is the key word. Scrolling on your phones in the same room doesn't count. Watching TV counts a little, but not much. What counts is doing something *together* — something that makes you talk, laugh, create, compete, or feel something.

---

## 7 Date Night Ideas That Actually Work

These are cheap, low-pressure, and genuinely fun. No fancy reservation required.

### 1. The Mystery Box Cook-Off

Each of you secretly picks three random ingredients from the kitchen. Mix them together (within reason) and both of you have to cook something using all six. You don't have to be a great cook. In fact, the worse the results, the better the story.

### 2. The Question Game

Print out or look up 20 deep conversation questions you've never asked each other. Sit outside with a drink and just... talk. Not about logistics. Not about the kids' schedules. About dreams, regrets, embarrassing memories, things you're afraid of, things you still want to try.

You'll be surprised how much you still don't know about the person you share a bed with.

### 3. Take a Class Together

Pottery. Salsa dancing. Painting. Axe throwing. Pick something neither of you is good at so you're both beginners. Being bad at something together is actually a bonding experience.

### 4. The "Yes Night"

One person plans the whole evening and the other person's only job is to say yes to everything. Then you switch next time. The planner gets creative. The follower gets to be surprised. It works better than you think.

### 5. Go Back to Where You Started

Drive past where you had your first date. Walk around the neighborhood where you used to live. Revisit the places that were part of your story *before* life got complicated. Memory is a powerful reconnector.

### 6. Build Something Together

Assemble a piece of furniture. Plant a small garden. Paint a room. Build a birdhouse. Working toward a physical goal together — with your hands, in the same space — creates a different kind of intimacy than sitting across from each other at dinner.

### 7. The Appreciation Dinner

No phones. Candles if you have them. Each person takes turns saying three things they genuinely love and appreciate about the other. Not surface stuff. Real things. Watch what happens to the energy in the room.

---

## The Real Problem (And the Real Fix)

The reason most couples fall out of the habit of dating each other isn't laziness or not caring. It's because they're waiting for the *perfect* conditions.

Waiting until the kids are older. Until things calm down at work. Until they have more money. Until they feel romantic again.

But romance is rarely something you *wait for*. It's something you *create* — with intention, with small gestures, with showing up for each other even when life is messy.

**The fix is not a vacation.** The fix is Tuesday night, no agenda, and your full attention.

---

## One Last Thing

If you're in Omaha and looking for inspiration — a new restaurant, a live music night, an outdoor event to explore together — our [events calendar](/events) gets updated every week with things happening around the metro.

Some of the best date nights start with a simple question: *"Want to try something new this weekend?"*

👉 **[See What's Happening This Weekend →](/events)**

---

*What's the best date night you've ever had? Drop it in the comments — someone reading this might steal your idea.*

articles.author: Explore Omaha
articles.title: The Secret to a Great Family Weekend (Hint: It Has Nothing to Do With Money)
articles.body:
## The Secret to a Great Family Weekend

Here's something nobody tells you when you become a parent:

Your kids don't remember the expensive stuff. They remember the *moments*.

They remember the time you let them stay up late to catch fireflies in the backyard. The random Saturday you said, "Let's go explore somewhere we've never been." The messy kitchen after you made homemade pizza together and nobody cared about the flour on the floor.

That's the secret. And the best part? It works in *any* city, any season, any budget.

Here's how to build a weekend your family will actually love — no spreadsheet required.

---

## Start With One Rule: Put Phones Away for Two Hours

Before you plan a single thing, make a deal with yourself. Two hours. No scrolling, no checking email, no "just one second" moments.

Kids notice when you're *actually* there. And when you are, something shifts. They open up more. They get sillier. They stop asking for the iPad because you're more interesting than the iPad.

Two hours of real attention is worth more than a whole weekend of being physically present but mentally somewhere else.

---

## The Creative Element That Changes Everything

Here's a simple idea that works for kids from age 3 to 13:

**Give them a camera — or your old phone — and a mission.**

Before you head out to a park, a festival, a farmers market, or even just a walk around the neighborhood, tell them: *"Your job today is to find five interesting things to photograph."*

Watch what happens.

Suddenly they're *looking*. Really looking. At the way light hits a puddle. At a funny-shaped leaf. At an old man feeding pigeons. At their little brother's shoelace coming untied.

At the end of the day, sit together and look at what they captured. Ask them why they chose each one. You will be genuinely surprised by what goes on inside their heads.

This one little activity turns an ordinary outing into an adventure — and it sparks conversations you never would have had otherwise.

---

## 5 Weekend Moves That Work in Any City

These aren't Omaha-specific. They work whether you live downtown, in the suburbs, or somewhere in between.

### 1. Find the Free Thing First

Every city has free stuff happening on weekends — outdoor concerts, library events, farmers markets, nature trails, community festivals. Start there. Free events have a different energy. People are relaxed. Kids can just *be* without the pressure of "we paid for this, so be having fun."

### 2. Let One Kid Be the Planner

Take turns letting each child plan Saturday morning. Give them a simple budget (even if it's "free things only") and let them pick. You might end up feeding ducks at a pond you'd never noticed, or at a playground that's actually pretty awesome once you get there.

This builds decision-making skills. It builds confidence. And it means *they* can't complain — because they chose it.

### 3. Do Something with Your Hands

Cooking together. Building something. Painting rocks. Making a birdhouse. Any activity where you create something tangible gives kids a deep sense of pride. "We *made* that." Hang it on the wall. Put it in the garden. Let it be imperfect and real.

### 4. Go Somewhere You've Never Been

In your own city, right now, there are probably five places you've driven past a hundred times and never stopped. A trail. A historic building. A small bakery. A neighborhood you've never walked through.

Make it a habit: once a month, explore something new within 20 miles of home. You'll start seeing your city through fresh eyes — and your kids will grow up knowing that *curious people are never bored*.

### 5. End the Weekend with a Family Question

At dinner on Sunday, go around the table and ask: *"What was your favorite five minutes of this weekend?"*

Not the best part. The best *five minutes*. It's specific enough that kids actually think about it. And the answers will surprise you every time. Half the time, their favorite five minutes was something you barely noticed — a silly dance in the car, a conversation at the breakfast table, a moment that cost nothing.

That's the reminder you need every single week.

---

## The Bigger Picture

Here's what all of this adds up to:

You are not just planning weekends. You are building your family's *culture* — the stories you tell, the traditions you keep, the way your kids learn to experience the world.

A kid who grows up going to farmers markets and exploring parks and making things with their hands? They become adults who know how to find joy in simple places. Adults who don't need to spend a lot of money to feel rich in life.

That's a gift that lasts longer than any toy or theme park ticket.

---

## Ready to See What's Happening This Weekend?

If you're in the Omaha area, we do the research so you don't have to. Every week, we update our events calendar with the best family-friendly things happening around the metro — free events, festivals, outdoor adventures, kids activities, and more.

👉 **[See What's Happening This Weekend →](/events)**

No subscription required. Just good stuff, curated for families like yours.

---

*Have a weekend tradition your family loves? Drop it in the comments below — we'd love to hear it.*

---

articles.body:
# Juneteenth JoyFest 2026: A Free Family Celebration of Freedom & Culture in Omaha

This weekend, Omaha comes alive with the **6th Annual Juneteenth JoyFest** — a vibrant, FREE family festival celebrating 161 years of freedom, Black artistry, and community pride. If you're looking for **things to do in Omaha with kids** that are meaningful, fun, and cost nothing, this is it.

## What You Need to Know

**Event:** Juneteenth JoyFest 2026 **When:** Saturday-Sunday, June 20-21, 2026 (Main event Saturday 2-10 PM) **Where:** The Union for Contemporary Art, Omaha, NE **Cost:** FREE (donations welcome) **Best For:** All ages — families, kids, teens, grandparents, everyone

## What to Expect: Movement, Migration & Celebration

This year's theme is **Movement & Migration** — exploring the journeys, resilience, and strength of the Black community. But don't let that intimidate you. This festival is joyful, colorful, alive, and absolutely family-friendly.

Here's what you'll find:

**Live Music & Entertainment** Local and regional artists perform throughout the day. Expect everything from soul to hip-hop, spoken word to gospel. The energy is contagious, and your kids will be dancing.

**Local Black Artists & Vendors** Support Omaha's Black-owned businesses and artists. Browse handmade crafts, art installations, jewelry, and unique goods you won't find anywhere else.

**Food Vendors** Come hungry. You'll find authentic soul food, Caribbean cuisine, BBQ, sweets, and more. This is peak food festival season, and Juneteenth JoyFest delivers.

**Kids Activities & Family Zones** There's a dedicated family area with interactive activities, games, and workshops designed for children. Your little ones get to celebrate, create, and learn about Black culture and history in a fun, welcoming space.

**Community Celebration** Juneteenth marks June 19, 1865 — when enslaved people in Galveston, Texas finally learned they were free. It's a day of liberation, reflection, and joy. At JoyFest, you'll witness real community — people gathering to honor freedom, support each other, and build something meaningful together.

## Why You Should Go

If you're a parent in Omaha searching for **free family events** that matter, this is your answer. Too many "family activities" are just sugar-coated commercialism. Juneteenth JoyFest is different. It's real. It teaches kids about history, celebrates Black excellence, supports local artists and businesses, and fills your weekend with actual joy.

Plus? It costs nothing. No ticket booths, no hidden fees. Just show up and celebrate.

## Pro Tips for Families

- **Bring a blanket or chairs** if you want to watch performances from the grass - **Sunscreen and water** — it's June in Nebraska. The sun is real. - **Cash for food vendors** — bring extra if you want to support local artists - **Start early** — arrive by 3 PM to beat the crowds and catch the afternoon performances - **Plan for bathroom breaks** — it's outdoors, so facilities may have lines

## Your Weekend Sorted

Looking for **family-friendly Omaha events this weekend**? Stop searching. Juneteenth JoyFest checks every box: it's free, it's fun, it's educational, and it's something your family will remember. Whether your kids are 2 or 12, there's something for them.

After JoyFest, you might also check out the **Mary Poppins musical at Hawks Mainstage Theatre** (runs through June 28) or catch a summer movie night at **Hanscom Park** later this week. But don't miss JoyFest. It's one of Omaha's best family events, and it happens just once a year.

---

**Ready to celebrate?** Head to [juneteenthjoyfest.com](https://www.juneteenthjoyfest.com/) for more details, or just show up Saturday at The Union for Contemporary Art. Your family is welcome. 🎉

**Join the Explore Omaha Newsletter** to get more recommendations for free and affordable family events happening in and around Omaha. New posts every day, straight to your inbox.

articles.title: You Are Not Behind — The Book Is Coming
articles.body:
# You Are Not Behind — The Book Is Coming

There is a specific kind of tired that nobody talks about.

It is not the tired that comes from working too hard. It is the tired that comes from wondering — quietly, in the back of your mind, while you are folding laundry or sitting in the school pickup line — whether what you are building is actually going to work.

You started something. Maybe a business. Maybe a side income. Maybe just a vision of a life that looks different from the one you are living right now. And most days you keep going. But some days, you wonder.

*This book is for those days.*

---

## What This Book Is About

"You Are Not Behind" is a collection of honest, practical, and deeply encouraging essays for parents who are building something — a business, a brand, a better financial future — while also raising kids, managing a household, and trying not to lose themselves in the process.

It is not a hustle book. It is not a get-rich-quick playbook. It is a book about showing up — imperfectly, consistently, and with purpose — even when you are scared.

Topics include:

1. Why money fear is not a character flaw — and what to do about it 2. The small financial wins that actually matter when you are starting from behind 3. How to build a business while being present for your family 4. What your kids are really learning when they watch you try 5. Legal and financial protection every family builder needs to know 6. The mindset shift that separates people who quit from people who make it

---

## Who It Is For

This book is for you if:

You are a parent trying to build a side income or small business and you are not sure it is going to work.

You wake up at 2 AM thinking about money.

You want to model something real for your kids — not just tell them to dream big, but actually show them what it looks like to try.

You have started something and you are still in the middle of it, and you need someone to tell you that the middle is not the end.

---

## Pre-Order Your Copy

The book is currently being written — and the content is being drawn directly from the real stories, real struggles, and real wins shared in this community.

Pre-order now at $19.97 and you will:

Receive the book as soon as it is complete

Get your name listed in the acknowledgments as a founding reader

Receive a free digital copy of the companion resource guide

Your pre-order directly funds the completion of this book. Every purchase is a vote that this message matters.

Pre-order here: https://buy.stripe.com/6oU28qbtS8iAawrdGFg7e0U

---

## A Note From the Author

I am writing this book because I lived it. I know what it feels like to be building something you believe in while not being sure the lights are going to stay on. I know what it feels like to look at your kids and want to give them more — not just money, but a story worth telling.

You are not behind. You are in the middle of something that is still becoming what it is supposed to be.

Keep going.

— Allan Pettit Founder, Explore Omaha

articles.title: Strawberry Picking with Kids: The Ultimate Omaha Summer Activity Guide
articles.body:
# Strawberry Picking with Kids: The Ultimate Omaha Summer Activity Guide

It's June 19th, and you have exactly **two days left** to take your family strawberry picking this season.

If that sentence made you sit up a little straighter, good. Nelson Produce Farm's beloved Strawberry Festival closes **June 21st**—and it's one of those experiences that looks simple on the surface but actually hits all the marks of what summer with kids is supposed to feel like: getting outside, getting your hands dirty, making memories, and coming home with something delicious to show for it.

But strawberry picking isn't just about the strawberries. It's the anchor for a whole day of summer activities that keeps your family busy, happy, and actually tired by bedtime (the holy grail of summer parenting).

Let's break down how to do this right.

## Why Strawberry Picking Actually Matters for Summer

Before you think, "Wait, it's just picking berries"—hear me out.

Strawberry picking does something specific: it gives kids a **sense of purpose**. They're not just being entertained. They're gathering food, deciding which berries are ripe, filling their own bucket, and solving the mild puzzle of "how do I pick this without squishing it?"

It's also one of the rare activities where a 3-year-old and a 10-year-old have equally useful jobs. Nobody's bored waiting for someone else. Everyone's picking.

Plus, there's something almost meditative about it. In a summer filled with screen time, sensory overload, and overscheduled activities, strawberry picking is **slow and intentional**.

And—let's be honest—there's nothing like fresh strawberries that your kid picked themselves. They'll actually eat vegetables for the rest of the week.

## Nelson Produce Farm: What to Know Before You Go

**Location:** 10505 N 234th St, Valley, NE (just outside Omaha)

**Hours:** Tuesday–Thursday & Sunday, 9am–5pm (Last entry: 4:15pm)

**Best Time to Go:** Early morning (9-10am) beats the afternoon heat. Bring a hat, sunscreen, and water—you'll be in the sun.

**What's Included:** - U-pick strawberries (pricing varies by how many you pick—typically $15-25 for a family basket) - Farm-fresh strawberry treats and jam available for purchase - Hayrides through the property - Photo opportunities throughout the farm - Petting areas with farm animals - Playground and open space for kids to burn energy

**What to Bring:** - Hat and sunscreen (non-negotiable) - Sunglasses - Water bottles - Wear clothes you don't mind getting stained - Closed-toe shoes (easier to walk in rows) - A small cooler with ice to keep berries fresh on the drive home

**Pro Tip:** The berries will be slightly cooler and less picked-over first thing in the morning. If you can't go early, go on a weekday rather than weekend.

## Turn It Into a Full Summer Day: Before, During, and After

Here's how to build strawberry picking into a complete summer memory, not just a pit stop:

### Morning (Before the Farm)

Start with a simple breakfast at home—nothing heavy. Then give kids a **mission briefing**: today you're going to become "strawberry hunters" and bring home berries for something special.

On the drive, play a podcast or audiobook episode together (something that makes them *think*, not just zone out—age-appropriate storytelling works great). This builds anticipation and actually engages their brains.

### Mid-Morning (Strawberry Picking)

Plan for **1.5 to 2 hours at the farm**. Pick for 45 minutes to an hour, then let them explore the petting areas and playground for the second half.

Take photos—not just of the berries, but of their faces, their hands, the field. These become the memories.

### Early Afternoon (After the Farm)

Stop for a simple lunch on the way home (or grab a farm treat). Let kids **choose one thing** they want to make with the strawberries—jam, shortcake, smoothies, chocolate-dipped berries. They're more invested if they choose.

### Late Afternoon (The Activity)

Make whatever they chose. If it's jam, they can help mash, stir, and taste-test. If it's shortcake or smoothies, same thing. This turns the strawberries from "we picked them" to "we made something with them."

## What Else to Do This Summer in Omaha (Nearby)

Since you're in Valley already, and you want to build a real summer plan, here are activities that pair well with farm trips:

- **Henry Doorly Zoo & Aquarium** (20 minutes away): The Kiewit Luminarium is incredible for STEM learning, and the zoo itself is world-class. Go once a month all summer. - **Bob Kerrey Pedestrian Bridge** (Omaha proper): Free, scenic, perfect for a family bike ride or walk. Good for clearing your head after a full day.

- **Omaha Central Library Summer Programming** (Free): Story time, STEM workshops, maker classes. Schedule one library visit per week in summer.

- **Scatter Joy Acres Petting Zoo** (Also in the Valley area): Another farm activity if your kids loved the animals at Nelson. Great for a less time-intensive outing.

## One More Thing: Build a Summer Rhythm

Don't try to do everything. Pick **three or four anchor activities** that repeat through summer:

1. One farm visit per month (strawberries now, tomatoes later, etc.) 2. One library visit per week 3. One big activity (zoo, museum, theater) every two weeks 4. One free outdoor thing weekly (bridge walk, creek exploration, park day)

This rhythm keeps summer from feeling chaotic while still hitting that "I did SO much" feeling by August.

## The Real Win

Here's what actually happens: In three weeks, your kids won't remember every detail of strawberry picking. But they'll remember:

- The taste of a berry they picked themselves - You being present and patient while they figured out how to pick them - Laughing together - Making something together afterward

That's the summer memory. That's the win.

**Nelson Produce Farm's Strawberry Festival closes June 21st.** If you haven't taken your family yet, this weekend is it. Go tomorrow morning before it gets hot. Bring a hat, sunscreen, and an open schedule. Make it matter.

---

**Want to stay in the loop on family activities around Omaha?** Join our weekly newsletter for event spotlights, activity guides, and hidden-gem tips that make summer with kids actually manageable. [Join here](#).

Already subscribed? Great—share this with another Omaha parent planning their summer!
